If a split is ambiguous, as in this case, or does not follow correct orthography, as is also the case, the patterns segtok uses do not indicate a split.

I.e., if you wish to use segtok on "noisy" texts with poor orthography, you might find yourself in trouble. 

Either clean up those cases prior to feeding data to segtok or you might be better of with a statistical splitter like Punkt, which tend to oversplit on text with good orthography.

Hope this advice helps you somehow and will close the issue.

Thanks for your work, and indeed as you've mentioned I've other priorities now - I'm working on https://gitlab.com/aiakos/aiakos. However, I believe I'll find some time to cleanup/merge all the PRs and release new version this or next month.

In terms of this feature - I strongly believe that translation data should be added to the DBus introspection data, maintained on the daemon's side of the pipe, and downloaded dynamically by pydbus and other dbus clients.

In case this is impossible (for example because you want it to work with old versions of daemons), there could be a language-neutral repo with this data, so that other DBus libs will be able to use it too. There are a lot of languages with DBus client libs, and I don't think doing the work in each of them standalone is a good idea. They should be as similar as possible.

There are links to multiple of them in the comments of https://github.com/LEW21/pydbus/issues/27 - you might want to discuss this topic with their maintainers, or on the dbus mailing list; they also might have more time for this than I have.

The sample code, as written tends to publish messages pretty fast and then exit.  If I slow it down by putting a sleep in it like this, it works:

    from curio import *
    from curio import zmq

    async def publisher(address):
        ctx = zmq.Context()
        sock = ctx.socket(zmq.PUB)
        sock.bind(address)
        for n in range(100):
            print('Not waiting anyone')
            await sock.send(b'Message %d' % n)
            print('Sent {}'.format(n))
	        await sleep(1)
        await sock.send(b'Message Exit')

    zmq.run(publisher, 'tcp://*:40000')

Seems likely to me that the subscriber never successfully hooks up with the publisher before it's done publishing the 100 messages (simply because it occurs so quickly).

So, here's how I get the list of domain controllers from a given domain based the existence of ldap SRV records in DNS.  From this, you can build a server pool by simply providing a single domain rather than a list of servers.  Note that this has not been heavily tested...

@cannatag
Giovanni, is this worth integrating into __ldap3.core.pooling.ServerPool__ such that it could be instantiated with just a domain name?

```
import logging
from dns import resolver
from socket import getfqdn
from importlib import reload

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def get_srv_records(service, protocol='TCP', domain=None):
    reload(resolver)
    local_domain = '.'.join(
        getfqdn().split('.')[1:]
    )
    query = '_{}._{}.{}'.format(
        service,
        protocol,
        domain or local_domain
    )
    records = []

    try:
        answers = resolver.query(query, 'SRV')
    except Exception as e:
        logger.exception('Query for {} failed.'.format(query))
        return records

    for record in answers:
        records.append({
            'host': record.target.to_text().rstrip('.'),
            'port': record.port,
            'priority': record.priority,
            'weight': record.weight
        })
    return records
```

So you just call the function and get back a list of domain controllers, hopefully suitable for a bind. You can specify the domain name or not.  If you don't, it attempts to get the domain configured on your machine via `socket.getfqdn()`.
```
>>> get_srv_records('ldap', domain='mydomain.com')
>>> [{'host': 'dc2.mydomain.com', 'weight': 100, 'priority': 0, 'port': 389}, {'host': 'dc2.mydomain.com', 'weight': 100, 'priority': 0, 'port': 389}]
```
Worth noting this only fails with Python 3.x; I would assume Python 2.7 is fine.

You're right in that the core functionality almost implements what Stopwatch does... and that you can implement such a check with only slightly more complication (one additional in-line computation).

The advantage of the `Stopwatch` is that it encapsulates the functionality that doesn't exist in the core Python, that of setting a start time and computing the time elapsed since that start time. It prevents errors that one might accidentally make, such as perhaps writing `start_time - time.now()` or accidentally comparing `time.time()` with `time.now()` or [race conditions that may emerge when the local timezone changes during the loop](https://github.com/jaraco/tempora/issues/3). The boilerplate logic (set a start time, calculate distance from start time) is replaced by a tried and tested implementation.

I agree it's a small advantage and that the cost of the dependency may outweigh the advantages in the abstraction. For this project, the cost of the dependency is negligible as the dependencies are pure Python and the Python packaging infrastructure makes the dependency declaration and inclusion a trivial matter. I do have to acknowledge, though, that other package managers may not find the costs of managing dependencies as trivial as Python does.

But moreover, since the behavior is used more than once in the package (twice), the advantages are multiplied. And what's more, the use of such a library demonstrates the value that such behavior would have in the standard library (as I've used the Stopwatch in dozens of other projects).

As I look at it more, I realize there's even more boilerplate logic in the code that's repeated in both functions:

- inference of seconds from datetime.timedelta.
- default infinite timeout
- comparison of elapsed time to timeout

This behavior should additionally be encapsulated somewhere, and the tempora package seems like a good place to do it.

So we see another advantage of incorporating such a dependency even for a simple usage as above - it drastically reduces the burden of factoring out functionality for re-use in other projects, encouraging maximal re-use and limiting repetition and divergence.

I agree about raising a HawkFail derivative rather than ValueError. That seems like a bug.

The problem you are running into is a problem of detecting the default configuration of `Receiver()`. By default, `Receiver()` is configured with `content=None` so it can't tell the difference when you pass it a `None` content from the user agent.

It could be fixed in a couple ways:

- You could modify your code to send an empty string when the content is None. I'm not positive that this will work but maybe.
- The default invocation of `Receiver()` could be changed to `content=EmptyValue` where `EmptyValue` is a custom class that can be used for comparisons.

> What if insert_multiple was deprecated or simply merged with insert which performed the isinstance check and automatically created a single instance array if isinstance returned false.

I think keeping `insert` and `insert_multiple` separate is a better and more consistent API. I really prefer having more single purpose methods than fewer multi purpose ones. In the end it's a trade-off between having too many methods, which is confusing, or having too few methods which do everything, which is confusing too.

I didn't check the actual implementation of chanselect and its thread-related semantics, but I like the general approach, when you pass getters and putters, and get channel that's ready.

The use of context here looks promising. Though the arguments of select must be operations (get/put) on channels, not just channels.

There's also [another attempt](https://github.com/rgalanakis/goless) at bringing Go-like functionality to Python. They took [`reflect.Select`](https://golang.org/pkg/reflect/#Select) for reference and implemented it in Python.

I tried to convert an [example](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Select_statements) from golang specification:

```python
c = Channel()
c1 = Channel()
c2 = Channel()
c3 = Channel()
i1, i2 = 0, 0

ch, value, ok = await select(c1.get(), c2.put(i2), c3.get())
if ch is c1:
    i1 = value
    print("received", i1, "from c1")
elif ch is c2:
    print("sent", i2, "to c2")
elif ch is c3:
    i3 = value
    if ok:
        print("received", i3, "from c3")
    else:
        print("c3 is closed")
else:
    print("no communication")
```

Methods `get()` and `put(v)` return Future subclasses that hold references to corresponding channels. Select must raise an error if *c2* has been closed prior, Go program panics in this case. *ch* is None when no channel are ready, i.e. default case, *value* is result of `get()`, *ok* is True when an actual value was received from channel, *ok* is False when channel was closed.

Docs of `reflect.Select` state that *ok* is "a boolean indicating whether the value corresponds to a send on the channel (as opposed to a zero value received because the channel is closed)." I'm very ashamed, but I don't quite get this passage :smile:

`Process.children()` is inevitably slow because it has to obtain `pid` and `ppid` for all processes:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/3a3598e433adec73e2a9c4d5f08e658516fb1d32/psutil/__init__.py#L862-L864
We can't get away from that.

With that said, it is true that we're doing more than just that because we also call `create_time()`, even more than once ([here](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/3a3598e433adec73e2a9c4d5f08e658516fb1d32/psutil/__init__.py#L330) and [here](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/3a3598e433adec73e2a9c4d5f08e658516fb1d32/psutil/__init__.py#L867)

We can probably avoid that and define a utility function similar to what we have on windows:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/3a3598e433adec73e2a9c4d5f08e658516fb1d32/psutil/__init__.py#L851-L854
...which just returns a `{pid; ppid, ...}` map for all processes in one shot.

import psutil

Shoot, I just realized the changes to `type` won't work. `type(str)` gets you back the real `type` which you can use to create new classes.

I think that's definitely a hole we want to close because `str.format` is dangerous if you can pass it custom objects. You can write things like 
```python
"{0[foo.__code__.__class__]}".format(x)
```
where `x` keeps a list of everything that has been passed to `x.__getitem__`. 

It might be more important to lock down `str.format`. For some applications you'll want that anyway, to prevent information leaks. Armin Ronacher's post [here](http://lucumr.pocoo.org/2016/12/29/careful-with-str-format/) inspired this attack, and it's something Jinja2 also has to defend against.

With DHCP it's a bit tricky. We have DHCPv4 support in pyroute2: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/master/pyroute2/dhcp/dhcp4socket.py
It does work: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/master/tests/test_dhcp.py#L11

But there is no state machine for DHCP (yet). So if you do not want to cope with raw DHCP protocol, you can use external implementations like dhclient:
```
from pyroute2 import NSPopen

nsp = NSPopen('test', ['dhclient', 'ex1'])
nsp.release()
```

Actually, the NLA data could be decoded using the fields framework, as for any other NLA type. The only custom code would be then to turn the trailing string into a list of IPs and vice versa:
```python
162     class seg6_encap_info(nla):                                                  
163                                                                                  
164         __slots__ = ()                                                           
165                                                                                  
166         nla_map = (('SEG6_IPTUNNEL_UNSPEC', 'none'),                             
167                    ('SEG6_IPTUNNEL_SRH', 'ipv6_sr_hdr'))                         
168                                                                                  
169         class ipv6_sr_hdr(nla):                                                  
170             __slots__ = ()                                                       
171             fields = (('encapmode', 'I'),                                        
172                       ('nexthdr', 'B'),                                          
173                       ('hdrlen', 'B'),                                           
174                       ('type', 'B'),                                             
175                       ('segments_left', 'B'),                                    
176                       ('first_segment', 'B'),                                    
177                       ('flags', 'B'),                                            
178                       ('reserved', 'H'),                                         
179                       ('segs', 's'))
```

Now the `decode()`:
1. run `nla.decode()`
2. load the IP list from the binary data and place into segs

The `encode()`:
1. fill all the required values
2. put the encoded list as a string into `segs`
3. run `nla.encode()`

The setup script shouldn't need to be invoked manually under normal circumstances. Rather, it's use will be building wheels for distribution through PyPI. The consensus also seems to be that the current installation process will be preserved, as it has various advantages.

Also, it should be noted that under normal circumstances there isn't any need to install acme-tiny in a virtualenv. Its only dependency is OpenSSL, so installing at the system level doesn't add any random packages or risk version conflicts.

I found an interesting edge case that can trigger unusually long polls:

- two consumers, different topic, same consumer id 
- processing loop that issues a poll() to one consumer, and then another

- something goes wrong and the program doesn't unsubscribe properly (or you're creating a new group).

The second poll() *always* would take 
``
max.poll.interval.ms
``
to subscribe, as if it was waiting for a member of it's group to expire. Note that this would happen, even if I waited hours between runs, and max.poll.interval.ms was set to a few minutes, so it doesn't appear to be waiting for the dead consumer to exceed max.poll.interval.ms. 

If I swapped the order in which I consumed, it would still block on the second poll() (even though it was on the other topic, that previously subscribed quickly).

And finally:

- putting the two consumers in different groups fixed the problem instantly. Even though one consumer was in the old group (I just appended a suffix to the previous group id for one of the consumers).

Note that in two functioning topics, where I had subscribed & unsubscribed previously, things were fine. The main problem was once it started failing, it never recovered.

The second poll() would *always* take max.poll.interval.ms, and cause the first to expire. 

Putting them in separate consumer groups seemed to fixed everything, even though I thought the same group id could be used across separate topics, and it would essentially be two different groups.

My recommendation would be to include everything you need to successfully build your project in the repo itself. It's true that this bulks out the repo, but the simplicity it buys you is enormous and these days storage is cheap and networks are fast.

The way I like to handle things with Heroku is first to disable the automatic collectstatic run by creating a file called: `.heroku/collectstatic_disabled`

Then I create a script in `bin/post_compile` which does everything necessary to build all my static assets (including run gulp, grunt, etc.) and then runs `collectstatic`.

This way I can run that command locally to test things and then be reasonable confident that when I push it to Heroku it will produce the same output.

Hope that's helpful.

i can't speak to correctness, but this is how [tootstream](https://github.com/magicalraccoon/tootstream) enables 2FA:

1. register the app with the instance (call `Mastodon.create_app()`)
2. instantiate a temporary Mastodon object with those credentials
3. display the login link (call `Mastodon.auth_request_url()`)
4. get the code from the user
5. get the auth token (call `Mastodon.login()` with the user's code) (no email/password here -- those are only needed for non-2FA)

if you store the auth token and app credentials, future runs can skip these steps and directly instantiate a logged-in Mastodon object.

Although my experience is largely centred on the micro:bit, it gives me some idea of just how complicated writing libraries for a cut down platform can get.

The key problems with MicroPython are:

* It's not Python, no matter how much it looks like it, it's a fundamentally different language that may omit features on the whim of the maintainer because;
* There's no singular MicroPython anymore, everyone's got their own flavour with slightly different device abstraction libraries.
* There is a low recursion limit/call depth based on the system stack size which, at least on the micro:bit, makes some levels of abstraction impossible

And, yes, Pillow wont help things!

A ground-up, MicroPython-specific version of luma with things like support for FRAM display buffers, and targeting one or two versions of MicroPython, and one or two devices with the requisite system resources could work, but I wouldn't be hopeful for direct support.

That said, my experience *has* been with the micro:bit which is MicroPython at its very "micro."

A really good example of point 1 is "slice attribute read access" on the micro:bit:

https://github.com/bbcmicrobit/micropython/blob/da7ce603ff18763f42aacc0318d1118c1a6a65d7/inc/py/mpconfig.h#L737-L741

They're disabled. This means "slice.start" and "slice.stop" don't work for apparently no sensible reason so I had to resort to.

```python
def _s(key):
    return (int(x.replace("slice(","")) for x in str(key).split(",")[:2])

start, stop = _s(slice_object)
```

Yes, you can grab the string representation of a slice object and get the start/stop from that but you can't access them as properties... :cry:

I was worried that the connection may get closed on calling `__exit__()`. This would create problems while implementing connection pooling.

For implementing my connection pool, I wrote a `class PooledConnection` as a wrapper around pyodbc connection. The `PooledConnection`'s  `__enter__()` & `__exit__()` methods call the `__enter__()` & `__exit__()` methods of the underlying pyodbc connection.

Now, I do want/need all the things that pyodbc connection's `__exit__()` method does, but definitely do not want it to close the connection.
In the `PooledConnection.__exit__()`, after calling pyobc connection's `__exit__()`, I would just mark the connection as free and "return" it to the pool.

So, basically, I count on `__exit__()` not closing the connection.

Thanks,

The ``noninjectable`` decorator[1] will tell Injector to not try to provide the value for a parameter and it'll let you do what you need:

```python
>>> import injector
>>> 
>>> class MyClass:
  2     @injector.inject
  3     @injector.noninjectable('title')
  4     def __init__(self, title: str = 'Default Title') -> None:
  5         self.title = title
>>> 
>>> injector_ = injector.Injector()
>>> injector_.get(MyClass).title
'Default Title'
>>> 
>>> builder = injector_.get(injector.AssistedBuilder[MyClass])
>>> builder.build(title='Doctor Who').title
'Doctor Who'
```

[1] https://injector.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#injector.noninjectable

Okay here is some interesting news :

Paramiko's proxy command is NOT python 3 compatible but it's only partially part of paramiko, because as a user you could give anything that is socket like to sock=, there is an issue about that : paramiko/paramiko#673 which contains a PR with a python 3 comptable version of ProxyCommand.

So to add the proxy command building helper in spur i would need a test, that both work in py2 and py3, py2 would use the ProxyCommand (stock) and the py3 would use a copy made from the PR.

So we *could* venture into creating a new ProxyCommand that works for both version and include it in your package, but i fear that would include some "if py2 then... if py3 then" in the code which is not the most elegant.
Or we could push to paramiko but that looks hard, there's 65 PR open and some are a years old.

My suggestion : leave it with the sock= i already proposed and no builders, so users such as me can have their own fixed proxy command that they use in their project that may not require py2 compatibility

I'm definitely not at all opposed to squashing commits -- that's even the workflow I follow for most of my projects both at work and on github, but you might not be aware that there's a little trick in git for automatically generating that squashed commit.  For example, once I'm ready to merge this branch, one can run --

```
git merge --squash keychain_authentication
git commit
```

and git will open up the default editor to show a generated commit message including all of the commits that will be squashed within.  Generally, it's standard practice to write a general description of the feature at the top, save the document, and when your editor closes, you'll discover your git history including that squashed commit.

That sort of thing, though, is best done after the pull request is fully-completed (that is -- as a step in lieu of clicking the 'Merge pull request' button).

@Ankit-Prajapati see the following from https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Basics_of_HTTP/MIME_types : 

```
application/octet-stream
This is the default value for a binary file. As it really means unknown binary file, browsers usually don't automatically execute it, or even ask if it should be executed. They treat it as if the Content-Disposition header was set with the value attachment and propose a 'Save As' file.

text/plain
This is the default value for textual files. Even if it really means unknown textual file, browsers assume they can display it.
```

Using `text/plain` will make your browser try to display it. Try to use `application/octet-stream` instead or some other MIME type that will prevent the browser from trying to display it and let another application handle it.

By "Electrum" I meant "ElectrumX". Corrected and sorry for the confusion.

The main difference is that it is currently not possible to only use Tor servers if a user wants to connect to more than one. The only option to connect only to hidden services is forcing Electrum (client) to connect to a single server, as described here: http://docs.electrum.org/en/latest/tor.html. So either a user chooses 1 particular hidden service (which is impractical due to Tor instability/overload sometimes), or uses "mixed" mode, connecting to hidden services and normal servers. 

Firstly, this is inconvenient. A Tor user cannot currently simply roam between hidden services without connecting to clearnet servers at all. No hidden server is able to return a Tor-only peerlist and some clearnet servers are always included. A Tor user is much more likely to choose a well known Tor server to connect to (some are hardcoded in coins.py)... only to receive another 6-8 clearnet servers in the peerlist from it. What's the point of that? Why can't a hidden service serve only other hidden services in its peerlists? If a user wants Tor, why involve clearnet at all?

Secondly, there is a practical security impact to this: A user connecting through Tor who chooses the plaintext TCP port assuming (correctly) connection remains encrypted because of how Tor works, if they don't force Electrum (client) to connect only to a single server, their connections to any clearnet servers, after leaving a Tor-exit, will travel further on in plaintext. This may sound trivial, but for example if a Tor-exit is compromised, this becomes an actual problem: https://www.torproject.org/docs/faq.html.en#CanExitNodesEavesdrop

Thirdly, it's impossible to run a dedicated hidden service (strictly Tor-only, no clearnet) enjoying all the possibilities ElectrumX provides, i.e. a) finding other hidden services (without trying to reach any clearnet peer), b) announcing itself only to other hidden services (again, without trying to reach any clearnet peers), and finally, c) including only .onion addresses in peerlists sent to clients. Or put differently, it is now currently not possible to create a Tor-only network of ElectrumX servers.

For example, my server is now connected to 111 peers, including only 24 Tor hidden services. When I connect to it (over Tor), I rarely receive more than one another hidden services in the peerlist. I end up forcing Electrum (client) to connect to the single server only. And if my broadband reconnects, or Tor circuit collapses, I end up disconnected. I'd love to be able to automatically reconnect to another hidden service **without touching any clearnet server**.

In overall, I imagine this so: An ElectrumX server operates as a strict hidden service (e.g. if TOR_ONLY variable is set), and contacts only other .onion peers, looking them up and announcing itself to them. It also accepts Tor-only client connections and provides the clients with only .onion peerlist. A Tor user this way simply selects a known Tor-only server and communicates only with other .onion servers, roaming between them if necessary.

Also I don't think configuration will be much more difficult because of this. It would be enough to make it conditional (tor_only would work only if e.g. force_proxy is set and report_host is unset). Best would be probably to make a new chapter e.g. "Tor hidden services" in environment.rst and describe all of this more plainly. I would be happy to do this for you.

I will give a birdeye view of chatterbot,

1. Did you [installed ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/setup.html#installation) chaterbot? If not please do so.
2. Second major import thing is, understanding of [chartterbot-corpus](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/corpus.html#chatterbot-corpus). In brief talks about [statements ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#statements) and [response ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html#responses) are in either  [JSON or YAML](https://stackoverflow.com/a/1729545/358458)
3. [Train](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/training.html) your bot.
4. Read some time on [logic-adapter](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/logic/index.html#logic-adapters) and how they are working. In short they are process your input requests.
5. After that where you are getting [input ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/input/index.html) and [output ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/output/index.html) to chat with your bot. i.e. [Linux/Windows Terminal](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/input/index.html#terminal-input-adapter), [Hipchat](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/output/index.html#id1), [Gitter ](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/output/index.html#gitter-output-adapter) or etc.

I may not be the best person to try, but here are the parts that jump out to me:

1. Explicit nesting of container types, through [`Nested` fields](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/nesting.html). (This contrasts with attrs, whose `.asdict` has a recursive option that relies on value introspection)
2. An adapter [ecosystem](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/wiki/Ecosystem) (and an API that enables it), so that translation/serialization approaches can be externalized and shared.

Other notable aspects I'm not as eager to preserve: 

1. The inheritance-driven approach (inheriting from `Schema`).
2. The separation of schema and container. Marshmallow works by serializing vanilla dictionaries and other objects handed to the schema's `dump` and `load` methods.

The last point is what enables Marshmallow to interop with attrs and other similar libraries, but it also leads to a fair amount of definition duplication in my limited experience.

Thanks for the report @JustAnotherArchivist.

The error message should actually read `... is not a valid sort field` (i.e. sort not search).

By default, search uses the Scraping API (http://archive.org/help/aboutsearch.htm). However, when you specify page and rows params, it uses the Advanced Search API (https://archive.org/advancedsearch.php). It looks like Advanced Search allows sorting by `oai_updatedate` while the Scraping API does not.

The reason both APIs are used is that the Scraping API doesn't allow paging, but Advanced Search does. Advanced Search does not allow exporting more than 10k results, but the Scraping API does.

It looks like this could be addressed with documentation updates and fixing the error message.

As a work around, I recommend dumping all of the results and sorting by `oai_updatedate` locally.

Thanks again for the report, and sorry for the confusion.

|-
| frame || frame || frame || Frame you want all of the HA widgets to go onto, if no frame is passed then a blank unnamed one is created.
|-
| columns || number || 1 || Display style, 1 is a one by one style, 2 will be 2 widgets per a row and so on
|-
| handler || table || nil || Handler for all callbacks to use, all callbacks require set/onSet/get to be passed as a string if this is set.
|-
| set || function/string || nil || The set callback that all widgets inherit
|-
| onSet || function/string || nil || The onSet callback that all widgets inherit
|-
| get || function/string || nil || The get callback that all widgets inherit
|}
;Remarks
You only need to pass the frame if you're doing some sort of custom modification to it, otherwise you can just skip it and it'll handle everything.

If you do pass a frame, do not rely on it being the one you should register with OptionHouse if your widgets are going to overlap off the OptionHouse display frame we wrap it a scroll frame around it.

===widgetData===
All HousingAuthority widgets (input, color, dropdown, slider, checkbox, button) accept these by default as fields so aren't listed.

;Fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| text || string || <required> || Text shown next to the widget
|-
| group || string || nil || Used for grouping widgets together
|-
| var || string/number/table || <required> || Used for identifying widgets, passed for all set/onSet/get/validate functions
|-
| help || string || nil || Shows a little "[?]" next to each widget in green, handy if you need to clarify or give extra information that can't be explained in the text alone.
|-
| handler || table || nil || Handler function for using in the callbacks require set/onSet/get to be passed as a string if this is set.
|-
| set || function/string || <required> || Function called when a widgets configuration value changes
|-
| onSet || function/string || nil || Same as set, however you should only use this if you need to know when something changes not changing the configuration
|-
| get || function/string || <required> || Called when we're loading the widget and when the frame is shown, the actual value of the widget
|-
| order || integer || 999999 || Order to display the widgets in
|}

;Remarks
'''Grouping'''

Grouping is a way to break up configuration so it doesn't look like a single mass of options, if one widget has the group field passed all of them must, the value should be the text that you want displayed, for example the below will show an input and color widget inside a frame labeled "Frame" and a check inside a frame labeled "General".

 { { type = "input",
     group = "Frame",
     <input data>
   }
   { type = "color",
     group = "Frame",
     <input data>
   }
   { type = "check",
     group = "General",
     <input data>
   } }

'''Callbacks'''
These are how the callback functions are handled along with arguments used.

''No handlers passed''

set(var, value)
onSet(var, value)
validate(var, value)
get(var)

''Handler passed''

handler[set](handler, var, value)
handler[onSet](handler, var, value)
handler[validate](handler, var, value)
handler[get](handler, var)

The problem get cause by plain Javascript. An Object key name in Javascript is not allowed to have a whitespace in it because if you have a $scope variable "test 2" you can't access it by using the Object decleration `$scope.test 2` but you could acces it by the associative array decleration `$scope["test 2"]`.
Unfortunately the angular $parser is using Object's, so it can't handle whitespace in key names.

Django does not store emails as case-insensetive because they aren't. According [RFC2821 ][1]

> The local-part of a mailbox MUST BE treated as case sensitive.  Therefore, SMTP implementations MUST take care to preserve the case of mailbox local-parts.  Mailbox domains are not case sensitive.

Many domains (like Gmail) handle email as though they are case-insensitive but it is not true that email addresses are case-insensitive.

[1]: http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2821.txt

For example, we log the raw XML request and response which may contain the full body of the emails requested, date and place for calendar appointments, etc. We would also need to look at exception messages.

Want to start off by saying love the lib 👍  am a huge fan of snapshot testing and this powers pretty much all of our python integration tests.

I agree around tests being deterministic, but just realistically our api's return db id's and I'd prefer not to mock out the whole db interaction bec it's kind of nice that it's captured by the snapshot test. our tests our deterministic outside of id generation from the db (sometimes things get committed/flushed for a bunch of reasons), so I don't think it necessarily makes sense to be overly dogmatic that a snapshot can't have an id field that changes. 

we have a bunch of code that cleans up our api responses to test that id's are returning correct values so that we can run them through the snapshot test. 

it would be really nice if there was someway to handle these situations smoothly and the tests would handle type checking values that are dynamic. so for example it would be great if in the snapshot we could do something like the following:

```python
self.assertMatchSnapshot(res, dynamic=[("id", number)])
```

where dynamic is a list of tuples specifying the dynamic ids, and the expected instance that should be asserted to match. I understand if this is a bit beyond the scope of the lib, bec of all of the different workflow that can happen around dynamic fields. That being said it could get pretty hairy to support the various workflows of dynamic json generation - alternatively you could say you only handle simple use cases.

@syrusakbary if we can agree on an appropriate api, I'd be happy to tackle and give back to the lib since it's been super helpful for us.

Currently the parser recognizes nicknames that are surrounded in parenthesis or double quotes. This is controlled by a regex, defined here: 

https://github.com/derek73/python-nameparser/blob/fb1475af3b939fcbdc679daf352d940376dac89e/nameparser/config/regexes.py#L26

And the parse_nicknames() method runs it: 

https://github.com/derek73/python-nameparser/blob/fb1475af3b939fcbdc679daf352d940376dac89e/nameparser/parser.py#L385-L395

You could update the regex to look for single quotes by replace that regex, something like: 

`
CONSTANTS.regexes.nickname = re.compile(r'\s*?[\(\'"](.+?)[\'"]', re.U))
`

I've found out the observer should be set to an attribute, and then it works:

```python
"""Test observer helpers."""
from sqlalchemy_utils import observes
import sqlalchemy as sa
engine = sa.create_engine('sqlite:///:memory:')
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
Base = declarative_base()
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)
import time


class Director(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'director'
    id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = sa.Column(sa.String)
    modified = sa.Column(sa.TIMESTAMP, server_default=sa.func.now(), onupdate=sa.func.current_timestamp())


class Movie(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'movie'
    id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = sa.Column(sa.String)
    director_id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, sa.ForeignKey(Director.id))
    director = sa.orm.relationship(Director, backref='movies')
    modified = sa.Column(sa.TIMESTAMP, server_default=sa.func.now(), onupdate=sa.func.current_timestamp())

    @observes('director.modified')
    def director_observer(self, modified):
        self.modified = sa.func.current_timestamp()

Base.metadata.create_all(engine)


def test_observer():
    """Test observer."""
    session = Session()
    director = Director(name='director')
    movie = Movie(name='movie', director=director)
    session.add(movie)
    session.commit()
    modified = movie.modified
    director.name = 'changed'
    time.sleep(1)
    session.commit()
    session.refresh(movie)
    assert movie.modified > modified

```

I agree with you on the colormap.
What is planned for color is these options:
 * shape is 0 dim, and it's a string, interpret as color
 * shape is 1 dim, items are strings, seperate color for each item
 * shape is 2 dim, items are strings, sequence of the above
 * no color scale / colormap is set
  * shape is 1 dim, items are floats, it should be of length 3 -> rgb values (possibly later 4 for opacity)
  * shape is 2 dim, items are float, it should be of shape (len(x), 3) -> rgb values
  * shape is 3 dim, items are float, it should be (sequence_length, len(x), 3) -> rgb values
 * color scale / colormap is set
   * shape is 0 dim, item if float, use the colorscale to map to color
   * shape is 1 dim, it's a seperate color for each item
   * shape is 2 dim, again a sequence

without having a default colormap/colorscale, it will try to interpret it as rgb values, when a colorscale is set, it will use that. For now you have to use matplotlib's colormaps directly (opacity is ignored):
```python
import ipyvolume
import matplotlib.cm
x = np.array([1,2,3,4,5])
y = np.array([2,4,3,2,1])
z = np.array([9,8,3,4,1])
c = matplotlib.cm.afmhot(np.linspace(0, 1, len(x)))
ipyvolume.quickscatter(x, y, z, color=c, size=10, marker="sphere")
```
I'd like to delay colormap/colorscale in ipyvolume until https://github.com/ipython/ipywidgets/issues/1153 takes more shape.

> uses data from "worksheet API call" and side_panel uses data from "bundle
> API" call, sometimes for a really quick job like "run date;" they would
> return different results even if the two requests are sent at almost the
> same time (one is 'running', the other is 'ready'). It's not deterministic
> either, I tried a couple times and sometimes table body and side panel are
> refreshed at the same time.
>
> What you saw is basically the "worksheet API" returns "running" but the
> "bundle API" returns "ready" so the side_panel will be updated and the
> table body will not. The table body will be updated, however, at the next
> fetch (maybe one or two seconds after, for these quick jobs).

Hi @ofajardo,

The imports need to be inside the function unfortunately if you are defining the function in the main script like this; the reason is that what is happening is the function is getting pickled and sent to the engines and it isn't capturing the Bio library loaded from the main script. If the function is part of a module then you don't need to do that.

The output from the print statements should show up in the stderr and stdout of the engine jobs. To capture the stderr and stdout you'd have to set up some custom logging to capture that. It is easiest if functions you are sending for computation are pure, so they don't have side effects like printing or writing out a file or anything, otherwise you end up having to debug parallel writing/printing issues and what not.

I'll add an option to remove -E for you all.

Moving forward, the plan is changing.  We want to extend settings beyond what CodeHilite provides, and to be honest, I don't even use CodeHilite to highlight indented code blocks (it's entire reason for existing).  I only every used it indirectly as Fences used it.  When I created SuperFences, I monkeyed Fences and did the same.  When I created InlineHilite, kind of an inline version of CodeHilite, I allowed the option of decoupling it from codehilite so you could set classes differently.  But I also gave you the options of controlling Pygments separately.  Why, I don't know.

So InlineHilite has a number of settings to configure highlighting separate from CodeHilite, but SuperFences does not.  Yet we want to add a feature that could extend highlighting options of both InlineHilite and SuperFences...this is a mess.

So what I think I will do is:

- Remove all pygment settings from InlineHilite.  You can set something like the class and turn off sourcing options from CodeHilite.  Eventually we will kill off CodeHilite sourcing all together, but not now.
- Create a new extension that configures highlighting globally.  Ideally this would just be an extension that handles inline and block highlighting and replaces InlineHilite and SuperFences, but SuperFences is coupled with things like UML as nested fences is such a complicated workaround that it was easier to shoehorn them in SuperFences than have a separate UML extension.  This makes it hard to just replace all highlighting extension as functionally, it isn't just highlighting, so instead we will just have an extension that configures highlighting for both extensions.  You can just toggle if you want too use CodeHilite or the more advanced version we will include.

This will be a big change, so this would force a 2.0 release.

It seems that django-chunked-upload is attempting to work around a bug in Django 1.4 where inner files were not being closed properly (https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload/blob/master/chunked_upload/models.py#L74-L76), but I didn't see an issue or much explanation about this change.

In my local testing, after closing the inner files, I am unable to re-open them again using `self.file.open()`.

Using `self.file.close()` and `self.file.open(mode='ab')` appears to work, at least under Django 1.8 with the default file based storage backend, so perhaps this fix is no longer required as Django 1.4 is ancient and has not been supported for a while now.

However, with `django-storages`, `self.file.close()` followed by `self.file.open(mode='ab')` doesn't throw an exception, but appears to not seek the file to the end (for appending).

@juliomalegria could you provide any guidance on what changes I might need to make to `django-chunked-upload` to support S3/boto backends via `django-storages`, and whether or not the Django 1.4 fix is still required?

I think that using S3 for storage is very common these days, especially as people are switching projects to Docker to make them scalable which generally requires a shared remote storage backend for media, so it would be great if `django-chunked-upload` supported that.

**UPDATE:**
Part of the problem is changes I make here are not viewable in the full site build pipeline as that checks out from specific branches on the main repo and not my fork. Then each legacy version only uses the configurations it has and not any updates I make here.
So... while I can fix the current build, the builds for every other version are still buggy as hell. :confounded:

I'm working on the tooling now so that you can supply logic from your fork/branch and supply that as an arbitrary version in the build. This process also is belabored by every version and every API build which makes for test cycles of about 40 minutes.  I'm updating the build tools to turn on/off documentation sets via a config file instead of how they're hard-coded in the Sphinx plugin file, mxdoc.py. I've got it down to 18 minutes for all versions.

This has led to certain realizations. 
:bulb: We're probably going to have to refactor the docs build process, so that we have better control.
:bulb: Each version should be built separately and maintained separately, rather than an overlapping pipeline.
:bulb: Each version should be built with configurations held in master, or... 
:bulb: We're probably going to have to make some commits to the old version branches to fix legacy bugs, so that we're not doing hot patches during every build. 
:bulb: Maybe we can cache old versions and not have to rebuild every time. Then builds can be done in a few minutes. 
:bulb: And finally, maybe I can actually debug whatever is going on with this Sphinx theme! Or if anyone really knows Sphinx theme customization and how this code works, they can help address why the left navigation behaves unexpectedly now that index files and table of contents are being supplied as per Sphinx's documentation. Basically, it seems to copy state from previous views and when you toggle between versions --> shenanigans.

Any help would be appreciated. :pray:

- In general the kvstore/optimizer API are broken because the shape of the NDArray on the server side is changed. This mean any optimizer that relies on the shape of the inputs (like FTRL, or any custom optimizer whose update depends on the shape). 
- On the other hand, this bug aborts the program when the shape is flatten to 1-D for large inputs, when the total number of elements is several billions.

@aoen Thanks for the feedback! (was trying to cc you on this PR but wasn't sure of your GH handle)

I am all for more separations of concerns between airflow core and the webserver, and ultimately for fleshing out the API to serve both CLI and UI. However, given the current state of Airflow API, we still have a long way to go.  For example, we probably want some discussions around:
-	Do we want to leverage the existing Flask framework or migrate to React.js or equivalent?
-	How is auth going to look like in the API world?
-	How do we maintain backward compatibility and versioning going forward?
On top of the discussions, implementing them will still definitely take some time. Putting RBAC on hold until API is done implies a major delay in introducing this feature.

That's why I propose that we migrate to FAB first, implement RBAC, and while this going on, we can work on freshing out the API. FAB exposes a list of JSON REST APIs on the models, so that could help with implementing APIs as well if choose to leverage it.

I understand that one of the major concern is maintaining two sets of wwws for a set period of time. I agree it’s not ideal, and I’m not a fan either, but assuming the community is okay with eventually deprecate the old www (or at least putting development effort on it to a halt), then we can aim to make this dual-www development process fairly minimal by merging it when it’s closer to being ready (not to mention there are still development needed on FAB to reach feature parity, it’ll likely take some time before it’s actually merge-ready).

My guess is that you aren't taking the `zmq_msg_size()` info about the length of the message into account. zmq messages are binary blobs, not null-terminated strings. So if you are using things like `printf('%s')` with the implicit assumption of null-terminated strings, then re-used memory (if it started out initialized to zero) will look like it always has data from longer messages from the past. But only the first `zmq_msg_size()` bytes contain valid data, the rest is undefined.

This will still cause KeyError exceptions. The safe way to check for prescence of a key in a dict is either:
`"summary" in params`
...or:
`params.get("summary")`

However I don't think we should repeat the error handling again and again. We can either

1) Try-catch around the `data` definition and use the KeyError exception to determine which key was missing, and use that in the error message (see stack overflow for how)
2) Same as [1] but just not bother making the message specific. "Invalid bug metadata" etc
3) Not doing a try-catch at all, but delegate any error handling to Bugzilla. ie: instead of eg:
```python
    'product': params["product"],
```
...use:
```python
    'product': params.get("product"),
```
And then Bugzilla will get `null` for that value and return a suitable error message, which the API will then return to the user. In the case of say `version` it may be allowed to omit it, so this might actually make sense. (Though whether Bugzilla counts `null` as not specifying a version is another matter, we may need to omit the key entirely).

(Another approach would be to use jsonschema validation, but given this API is effectively a thin pass-through layer to the Bugzilla API, whose validation requirements are not overly visible to us, I don't think this makes sense.)

With either approach I also think we should rename the property `description` passed by the UI to `comment` to make it clear that it's not the actual comment that will be posted (since the username is prefixed). In addition, the construction of the `description` field should use `.format()` and probably be outside the `data` definition for readability.

> Also, is there any way to log to the server the instances where it's passing an empty summary? 

You can use the Python logger, though I don't think we want to log anything in this case. (Aside from when you're developing locally).

Hi Chris,

Thanks for the comment. Right now, I'm overriding the `complete` method and checking the dependencies recursively. As you pointed out, this task is indeed non-trivial in terms of the efficiency. What I have now is an O(n!) complexity way of doing it, and the overhead can be significant when the number of tasks gets large. I wonder how GNU Make deals with this, maybe it just assumes no source code should contain millions of source files...

However, I think this is a very useful feature. Even in the production runs, if I accidentally passed in one wrong input data file to one task, I certainly don't want to re-do all the tasks, but rather re-run the "affected" tasks. However, to manually find out and delete the invalid outputs is a very annoying thing. And handling all these dependencies and update the out-of-date outputs is one of the most important features for a task manager like Luigi. 

This feature may not be turned on as default, but I would like to see this as an option.

Hi, there's not really a lot of data for us to go on here.  What I would suggest is to enable debug logs (`boto3.set_stream_logger()`) and see what's taking long.  Just a few possibilities I can think of:

1. You're creating a new process per item in your `conversation_data`.  If that list is large you're potentially spinning up a larger number of processes.  I would suggest using `multiprocessing.Pool`.
2. It's possible you're getting throttled by the service.  This will show in the debug logs.  If that's the case there's not much you can do from the boto3 side, it's the service that's throttling.

Also, just as a side note, I wanted to double check you have the right args here.  You're `args` is passing the conversation and child connection:


```
    process = Process(target=detect_conversation, args=(conversation, child_conn))
```

but your handler has:
```
def detect_conversation(self, conversation):
```

The `conversation` arg seems to be swapped.

If you're still having issues, please attach a debug log and we can take another look.

So this behavior looks like it is being sourced from the server. I would recommend opening up a thread on the [AWS Developer Forums](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=30) to get a clarification is of the appropriate behavior. All boto3 is doing is taking your parameters, serializing them into an HTTP request, parsing the HTTP response, and returning you the data. It does not modify the response to limit the number of items returned to you with this interface.

If I were to guess, the ``MaxResults`` parameter does not guarantee that you will have 50 results. It only guarantees that you will not have more than 50 results.

Hi, main point of this issue is to be aware of it. 

mzML and mzData do support sample names, e.g. [MTBLS338/Bur-189_2-D,5_01_37808.mzData](ftp://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/metabolights/studies/public/MTBLS338/Bur-189_2-D,5_01_37808.mzData) has `<sampleName>Bur-189</sampleName>`. Bruker instruments automatically add the autosampler position `2-D,5` and serial number of the injection `37808` to the assay name. 

Similarly, [MTBLS341/pos-Exp1-Blatt_K-4_1-B,4_01_8086.mzML](ftp://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/metabolights/studies/public/MTBLS341/pos-Exp1-Blatt_K-4_1-B,4_01_8086.mzML) was converted with `<software id="exportSoftware" version="3.0.9">` and 
has `<sample id="sample" name="pos-E495-Blatt_K-4">`

Yours, Steffen

Hello @dainnilsson!

Yes. You understand correctly.

In yubicommon 

```python
def byte2int(i):
    if _PY2:
        return ord(i)
    return i
```

Python3 supports ord as well, so we can just remove Python2 checker

```python
def byte2int(i):
    return ord(i)
```

And then in python-u2flib-server, in RawAuthenticationResponse, we can simply
```
self.user_presence = byte2int(data[0:1]) << 7
```

My apologies to bean so rude. Now reading my comments I realize that despite the info I provided and some of my options are still valid (IMHO), it's wasn't appropriated the way I wrote what I wanted to say.

I understand that the issue tracker is most a place where to discuss bugs and improvements, and discuss whether this is a bug or an error by design is not clear in the thread, but again my apologies.

I thing there are two strong topics here:
- Should pipenv update all your outdated dependencies where you are trying just to install a new dependency: the ones that are not needed to update because the new package / version we are trying to install can works with the existent dependencies, and even the ones that aren't dependencies of the new package we are trying to install? Maybe this is out of scope of this ticket, but it's a really important topic to discuss.
- Do one of these parameters `--keep-outdated` `--selective-upgrade` allow us to avoid these behaviour? It's not clear what these options do, there is a lack of documentation about them, and even in the related issue (#1554) nobody is answering that.

In case it's a bug in on one of these params `--keep-outdated` `--selective-upgrade`, I still thinking that do not set whatever param solves the unnecessary update of the dependencies as default it's a really bad idea.

To compare with a similar scenario, imagine that you execute `apt-get install vim` to just install the `vim` tool in your system (and the necessary vim's dependencies or updates if apply), but imagine also that in this situation apt updates all the other dependencies of your system: python, the QT system, the Linux kernel... and so on. It's not that apt shouldn't allow us to updates other dependencies, but there is a clear command to do that: `apt-get upgrade`, while `apt-get install PACKAGE` just install / update PACKAGE and it's dependencies.

However, as you've noticed, only the wheel got uploaded and the error caused the process to fail to upload the other dists (tar and zip). I suspect this issue is also causing problems with the bootstrap script which will attempt to get the zip archive.

I'm +1 on this initiative, but I do think it would be nice in the documentation for `register` and `upload` if we actually provide the specific 1-to-1 equivalents in addition to just linking to the `twine` documentation. I often find that it's way easier to move over when I don't have to learn a whole new tool just to figure out how to do the equivalent of what I used to do with the old tool.

Also, ideally this would have tests, though I'm not super familiar with the `announce` mechanism used here and how easy it would be to test that. Maybe the best thing to do is to use a mock of some sort to make sure that `announce` is called with some regex matching something like `.*[Rr]egister.*deprecated.*twine.*` (or matching all three patterns `[Rr]egister`, `[Dd]eprecated`, `twine`).

While working on this fix one should keep in mind that unlike solution proposed in `dateutil` repository we would want to *seamlessly* replace day if it exceeds days for the resulting month, but *ONLY* if it is valid date. @puzrin Size isn't the only performance consideration, there's also speed/latency. That's one of the reasons I picked YAML over alternatives like plain old JSON because it's syntax was built to support fast stream parsing and the ability to deal with chunks (documents) with somewhat low latency, parsed relatively quickly as soon as they come in. My mistake was in assuming the most well know implementation in this language was designed for that (as the spec certainly encourages it). Certainly I can do my own parsing (chunking input on my own from the stream by regexing around for `---` and `...` and some more complicated things), but it seems silly to me to wrap a parser in a mini-parser rather than just fixing the parser.

IMHO that's a madness to use yaml parser/dumper such way :). It's not intended for URL encoding. Goal of dumper is to create pretty human-readable result. May be it creates something close to your wishes... by accident... but that's not enougth to add as official option.

I tend to reject as "out of project scope", but if i miss something important, please explain.

Any breaking dependencies in `mock` shouldn't be an issue for developers using this library, since it's only installed in dependencies for testing this package.

For `requests` though, you're right that a developer might be surprised by a change in the API, but if they're explicitly using certain features in the response object that is returned, it would be their prerogative to pin a version of requests that works for them.

Consider also that if we pinned `requests`, it would _prevent_ developers of this library from using the latest version, even if they were okay with the new changes.

About a quarter of the time is spent in `random.choice` in `partitioners.py`.  Another decent chunk is spent in protocol.py setting a timestamp.  There is another large chunk in `producer.py::produce` that is hard to observe well because it's not in function calls.  I did a tiny bit of micro-optimization and shaved off around 10-15% from the total time

```diff
diff --git a/pykafka/producer.py b/pykafka/producer.py
index b3aa5e8..2aff29a 100644
--- a/pykafka/producer.py
+++ b/pykafka/producer.py
@@ -317,13 +317,13 @@ class Producer(object):
         :return: The :class:`pykafka.protocol.Message` instance that was
             added to the internal message queue
         """
-        if not (isinstance(partition_key, bytes) or partition_key is None):
+        if partition_key is not None and type(partition_key) is not bytes:
             raise TypeError("Producer.produce accepts a bytes object as partition_key, "
                             "but it got '%s'", type(partition_key))
-        if not (isinstance(message, bytes) or message is None):
+        if message is not None and type(message) is not bytes:
             raise TypeError("Producer.produce accepts a bytes object as message, but it "
                             "got '%s'", type(message))
-        if self._protocol_version < 1 and timestamp:
+        if timestamp is not None and self._protocol_version < 1:
             raise RuntimeError("Producer.produce got a timestamp with protocol 0")
         if not self._running:
             raise ProducerStoppedException()
```

(isinstance checks can be surprisingly painful when done in inner loops)

If there are cases where the metadata reports replicas that are not in our `Cluster.brokers` then we will of course fail here with an uncaught `KeyError`. That is not a regression however, because previously we would fail in a worse manner, raising an incorrect `LeaderNotAvailable`.

Ideally we need to be able to produce scenarios that bring about a replica `KeyError` but this could fall under the work for #347.

                              config={'LD_LIBRARY_PATH': '/path/to/librdkafka'}})
```

I noticed that last bullet point recently as well and thought of bringing it up here.  The problem is, as you say, it requires a recent version of setuptools.  What one might be able to do is move that functionality to astropy-helpers (if it hasn't been already) and just enable it when needed for backwards-compatibility.  Then most package maintainers will almost never have to touch setup.py itself which is a good thing.
I switched over for my other projects. Never had problem. It is just changing the extension setting. One less dependency to worry about.

To add a little more background, setuptools comes with its own [test](https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/src/e517fced669d54bcaf3da2d8ca468d29120fedfc/setuptools/command/test.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default) command, which knows, to a limited extent, how to locate and run tests that use the standard library `unittest` module.  But it's typical to override this command to support other test frameworks (like py.test).

It's only setuptools' built-in `test` command that does anything explicitly with the `test_requires` setup argument.  I see no reason this couldn't be supported in the `test` command in astropy-helpers though. > At the end of the day, I think the power of scikit-image will be to position itself as a powerful library for image processing, as opposed to metadata handling. I am of the mind that documentation and keywords like axis to find where the rgb axis is, are basically all we need for now.

This is my position too. @jni accurately summarized my misgivings about breaking return type stability above. A more serious concern I have is that correctly propagating metadata through, and updating it accurately to reflect the action of a given function, is a hard problem. It also makes it harder to immediately see what a function's inputs are. If a function accepts a dictionary of metadata, how do I know which keys in that dictionary affect the function's behavior and which are just being passed through (and maybe updated)? I think it better to force the user to handle these things and pass in exactly what a function needs, so both the author and future readers of the code will understand what the dependencies are, i.e. what choices are effectively being made.

To add onto @stefanv and @jni's remarks above about a `.meta` attribute: we started down that path in [pims](https://soft-matter.github.io/pims/v0.4/) with `Frame.metadata` and we quickly regretted it, not least of all because functions from external libraries that users might apply to their images (e.g., `numpy.sum`) do not know about this system and cannot participate in it.

Similarly, the suggestion of capturing the "processing history" as part of this worries me. Many projects in the workflow-management/provenance-capturing space have tried to do this with mixed results, and I think it would be risky for scikit-image to attempt to adopt this goal as part of its core functionality.

But that doesn't mean scikit-image can't do _anything_ to help. As a concrete step, would it make sense for the reading functions to return a `image, dict_of_metadata`, adopting canonical skimage names in the process? This would enable users to write lossless (or less lossy) converters, to start.

@surabujin, perhaps you're confusion is with what "deserialize" does...  Colander has no concept of JSON and it's serialized format is an internal-use format called "cstruct" which is a dict that contains only strings.  "deserialize" is the process to translate the cstruct into a dict with more "normal" types (such as integers) or custom types and will throw exceptions if it encounters errors.

So, the process of validation (as far as I understand it) is like this:
1. JSON -> simplejson -> basic dict
2. basic dict -> colander serialize -> cstruct (dict with only strings)
3. cstruct -> colander deserialize -> basic dict that may contain custom types

Any of those steps can throw an exception which indicates the validation failed.  The colander steps will also make appropriate calls to validators and preparers.
Obviously, this is simply better.

So, it took quite some time, but since my master thesis is finished now I would like to talk about this pull request in a more pragmatic way:

For the sake of my thesis I introduced some generality and features which, after some experimentation, turned out to be not needed to solve the problem that originated this work.

It became apparent that it is easier to pass the references or author lists as a whole to a resolving function, which means that we don't need to introduce another difference algorithm which is based on some kind of sequence extraction and that the current difference extraction process for lists is suitable enough or will never be used in our case.

This means: We could introduce the possibility for custom extraction algorithms just for the sake of it and risk that it breaks some users code without providing us any value. Even though this pull request does not break the usage of the dictdiffers API, it seems quite possible that it might break some code because it introduces an order to the patches, which changes their layout a bit (Each deletion or insertion patch only contains one element in the list of changes (third element of the tuple)).

As I told @kaplun, I am currently figuring out how much time and effort it would take to make the merging process work with the current style patches. Up until now it is mostly about getting rid of the most tedious code changes.

So, comments? Do we want to give users the option to use their own extraction algorithms or not?

My main motivation is described over in zopefoundation/zope.lifecycleevent#11. It might be good if its high-level APIs (like `modified(object)`) were able to return the event that they created and sent. There's some logic in those APIs that does things like attempt to automatically determine the `__parent__` and `__name__`, and it might by nice to be able to document and demonstrate that behaviour directly (without the layer of indirection of setting up subscribers)---it also reinforces the notion of exactly what those APIs are doing.

Having `notify` return its event is the simplest, fastest way to be able to have `modified` et al return *their* event, because they simply create it inline in the call to `notify`: `return notify(ObjectCreatedEvent(object))`. I can imagine it being helpful for similar reasons elsewhere, although I don't have any concrete examples handy.

This isn't critical functionality for me, just a nice-to-have, and I won't be upset if it gets vetoed. At the same point in time, I can't see that it does much damage either (leaving aside the doctests, which are just inherently fragile).

Well, believe it or not, the close behavior is actually by design. :( See the "Caution" note on the Android Activity lifecycle about how it closes and restarts the app each time the app changes rotation:

https://developer.android.com/training/basics/activity-lifecycle/recreating.html

I guess completely reloading the app is one way to ensure it will load properly in the current rotation.... ;-) Doesn't explain the crash, but perhaps it relates to the app closing at an unexpected time? One other weird thing is that it seems Android has a Bundle instance object which saves certain app state and restores it automatically, and if it only partially restores the state or the app isn't expecting Android to do any restoration, it may be creating weird effects.

I am creating an 7-Zip plugin for brotli. Currently there seems to be no special magic for testing of brotli's presence in some data.

I found some first definitions of a frame format v3 in the brotli repository of @madler ... but that's it.

For my 7-Zip extension, the stream with the container will be defined like this:
- 12 bytes skippable frame
  - 4 bytes magic id 0x184D2A50U
  - 4 bytes length of the skippable frame (always 4)
  - 4 bytes with the length of the following compressed data, CLEN (including 4 bytes magic)
- 4 bytes magic id in the beginning of compressed data: "BROT"
- CLEN bytes compressed data stream to be decoded

But I could also support "normal" brotli streams, if I can recognize them.

AFAIK, Igor will not add new codecs to Standard 7-Zip currently.

Having thought more about this, I'm inclined to keep the "command" argument rather than replacing it with "args". We'll make it accept both a list of args or a string. It does after all represent the Fire _command_, and while args would also be a suitable (perhaps better!) name, I've come to think that the backwards compatibility argument outweighs this.

[Relatedly, there is also precedent for accepting a command both as a string or a list of arguments ([subprocess.py](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/subprocess.py#L548)), though they use the name args.]

2.

> I also need to think more about what name + usage strings should look like if you call Fire from inside a function that was already called by Fire (as we will likely do in #29).

If you call Fire with:
- no command and no name -> command=sys.argv[1:], name=sys.argv[0]
- no command and a name -> command=sys.argv[1:], name=name
- command and no name -> command=command, name=sys.argv[0] or None(?)
- command and name -> command=command, name=name

The third case (command is supplied but name is not) is the most interesting one. I'm inclined to use sys.argv[0] in this case, but am also considering removing the name from the usage strings altogether in this case.

3.

When calling Fire from a method launched with Fire, one will either:
a) want access to the original unparsed arguments,
b) want Fire to not further parse the arguments.

We unofficially enable a) with the decorator in decorators.py "@SetParseFn(str)", but we'll want to make it official for this to be useful. We don't support b.

(Although we support +, but in lexical space:

Basically, the docstring in ```view.neuron``` says that one has to use the ```limits``` key argument to pass the limits of the axes. This key argument should propagate through ```view.neuron``` to ```view.common.plot_style```. This is not happening because ```plot_style``` uses the key arguments ```xlim```, ```ylim``` and ```zlim``` for the respective limits and not ```limits``` option itself.

In other words, the ```view.neuron``` docstring needs updating in order to reflect the actual options that can be passed in the function (i.e. that ```xlim```, ```ylim```, ```zlim``` are used to limit the axes, not the option ```limits```).

Hello,
the error that you get is the following from pylint (you can see it by clicking details):
```
Cannot decode using encoding "ascii", unexpected byte at position 50 (invalid-encoded-data)
```
I am not an encoding standards expert but the general idea is that the string is decoded assuming an ascii encoding, which is the default behaviour, while your special unicode characters are in UTF-8 encoding or something similar and thus the ascii decoder does not understand them.

Different encodings must be handled with care in order to make sure that you will not run into surprises! 
Check the following link if you want to see a simple explanation on the different encodings:
http://nedbatchelder.com/text/unipain/unipain.html#1

Now, as far as the change is concerned it is in general better to avoid special encoding, unless your application needs to process it. Thus, you can change it to um and um2 respectively.

Hope that this helps :)

But SlugRelatedField return slug, PrimaryKeyRelatedField return pk, HyperlinkedRelatedField return hyperlink, look at [this](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/relations/#hyperlinkedrelatedfield) and etc. SerializableRelatedField return serialized object - not just one entity like string(in case of SlugRelatedField, HyperlinkedRelatedField) or integer(in case of PrimaryKeyRelatedField). Prefix say what we have in representation. It's return a serialized structure. PresentablePrimaryKeyRelatedField doesn't return PresentablePrimaryKey it return serialized structure, so i think, it's a not good name.

2. It's not for representation of object, it's made to valid if related field with this pk exists or not. Look at [source code of PrimaryKeyRelatedField](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/relations.py#L219). It's done analogical, but without `pk_field` checking.

3. I don't think, that it's right place for this idiom, because in use default behaviour that I'm write in docs, and if you pass ModelSerializer in some cases there is no need to pass queryset, because you know model of ModelSerializer, if you want to pass special queryset, you can pass queryset, and if you want with special manager.

4. Extending from PrimaryKeyRelatedField is bad, because it can take `pk_field` argument, that represent object, [look at the docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/relations/#primarykeyrelatedfield), but you don't use it in PresentablePrimaryKeyRelatedField.

# Branch that is modified going to schema B

  failure_nodes:
  - job_template: workflow JT2
  - job_template: workflow JT
  success_nodes:
  - job_template: workflow JT2
  - job_template: workflow JT

# Branch that is deleted going to schema B
- project: Ansible Examples
  always_nodes:
  - inventory_source: tower script1 (workflow demo
  - inventory_source: tower script2 (workflow demo
```

Schema B

```yaml
# Branch that has deeply nested content removed  going to schema B
- job_template: workflow JT
  failure_nodes:
    - inventory_source: tower script1 (workflow demo
  success_nodes:
  - project: Ansible Examples
    always_nodes:
    - job_template: workflow JT

# Branch that is modified going to schema B
- job_template: workflow JT2
  failure_nodes:
  - inventory_source: tower script1 (workflow demo
  - inventory_source: tower script2 (workflow demo
  success_nodes:
  - project: Ansible Examples
  - job_template: workflow JT
```

This feature seems to be able to swap back and fourth between A and B. I see DELETEs and POSTs right where I expect them. Reapply either A or B after that state has already been reached, and it makes only GET requests, no changes are done.

I have actually avoided the indeterminate cases where 2 of the same JTs are on the same level next to each other. I would recommend that schema authors just avoid that, and my example schemas will, thus, follow the rule as well.

Also, we can't really have any PATCH actions with the approach to schema authoring I'm testing. I presume we would if the ids were used, but I would also hesitate to directly suggest that method as a reusable and sharable format.

On Thu, Feb 23, 2017 at 9:05 PM, Alexander Kukushkin <
notifications@github.com> wrote:

> What would happen in this situation:
>
>    1. All standbys die
>    2. Two standbys return
>    3. Writes happen
>
> That's fine. Write can always happen when in DCS there is no information
> about synchronous replica.
> You can't prevent it. First we set synchronous_standby_names and only a
> few moments later update DCS. This operation can't be atomic.
>
>
>    1. Master fails
>    2. Failover
>
> Failover will not happen, only old leader is allowed to be promoted,
> because of DCS.
>
> When using * there is a potential for data loss, when using dummy I don't
> think there is?
>
> Once again, '*'will be used only when we know that there are no replicas
> available.
> At the same time in sync key in DCS will contain {"leader": "leader-name"}
> .
> That means failover will not happen. Only the old master is allowed to
> become a leader.
>
> Table of state transitions (hope it will help you to understand how it
> works):
> connected replicas synchronous_standby_names DCS comment
> *r2*,r3 r2 leader: r1, sync_standby: r2 two replicas are connected, r2
> synchronous and it is also declared as a synchronous in DCS, therefore on
> master failure it will take over
> None r2 leader: r1, sync_standby: r2 both replicas are dead, but in DCS
> r2 is still declared as synchronous. If the master will fail, only the old
> master or r2 are allowed to be promoted
> None r2 leader: r1, sync_standby: None Patroni removed sync_standby: r2
> from DCS. From this moment on master failure only the old master can be
> promoted.
> None * leader: r1, sync_standby: None both replicas are dead. If the
> master will fail - nobody will take it over except the old master. But if
> the new replicas will come, one of them will become synchronous.
> r2,*r3* * leader: r1, sync_standby: None two replicas are connected, r3
> synchronous, but not in DCS. Writes are possible, but on master death
> nobody will take over.
> r2,*r3* r3 leader: r1, sync_standby: None two replicas are connected, r3
> synchronous, but not in DCS. Writes are possible, but on master death
> nobody will take over.
> r2,*r3* r3 leader: r1, sync_standby: r3 two replicas are connected, r3
> synchronous and it is also declared as a synchronous in DCS, therefore on
> master failure it will take over

> How did you discover that `'amount'` in bracket is faster then `hasattr(bracket, 'amount')`

By profiling and discovering that weirdly a lot of time was spent creating `ParameterNotFound` errors, which didn't think to make sense. 

I turned out that each time, `hasattr` calls `__getattr__`, which raise a `ParameterNotFound` if the attributes doesn't exist, and catches it under the hood (as it's a subclass of AttributeError). That's more work than just checking if the item is in a list. 

I also found [resources](https://hynek.me/articles/hasattr/) indicating that `hasattr` is generally not a good option.

I think exporting `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH ` should solve the problem. One thing Mac users need to pay attention to is __Disable System Integrity Protection (rootless)__ before exporting this environment variable. To verify this, try `env | grep DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`. 

To turn off, follow below steps:
1. Boot OS X into Recovery Mode(hold down Command + R keys simultaneously after you hear the startup chime)
2. When the `OS X Utilities` screen appears, `Utilities`->`Terminal`
3. Type `csrutil disable; reboot` into the terminal then hit return.
