500 lines of code?  The 568 lines in the patch are 90+ lines of docs, 310+ lines of unit tests, ~30 liines of module boilerplate, and 130 lines of "real" code. Those 130 offer a working proof-of-concept that a layered approach is workable.  We can argue about the merits of proxies (DRY, easier mainteinance) vs. explicitly wrapped APIs (better tooling support?), but it *is* possible to offer the pattern as an option, without imposing it willy-nilly on all uses of the library.

The discussion we *were* having seemed to me unresolved / ungrounded -- it was all based on comparing tiny snippets, without being able to compare how the new pattern would look in *real* applications.  I picked `pubsub` as a sample, because it was the simplest, and wrote this PR to show that we could offer both patterns in a layered approach, including being able to compare real-world examples of both.  For instance, *both* gists I posted earlier today will work with the code in this PR.

```python
from gcloud import storage
b = storage.get_bucket('name')
```

which is equivalent to

```python
from gcloud import storage
b = storage.Bucket('name')
b.reload()
```

It does clarify my question, thank you.

I see how this is a useful feature in principle, although when I have time to implement it is a separate question. The Pub/Sub team is primarily concerned with high throughput situations -- they actually explicitly wanted "polling" pull to be _explicitly removed_ so that people would not use it by accident.

There are a few options here.

First: You could use the old polling-based `pull` method. It is not present in the documented surface, but you can get at it: `from google.cloud.pubsub_v1.gapic.subscriber_client import SubscriberClient`. That class (which should look _mostly_ like `google.cloud.pubsub_v1.subscriber.Client` has a `pull` method that will perform the old polling behavior.

Second: You could implement the batching yourself. In fact, it would probably look pretty similar to the batching class we provide on the publisher side. Here is a quick pass at a plausible implementation (note: written in the GitHub text editor; may have some silly bugs):

```python
class Batch(object):
    def __init__(self, max_messages=250, max_seconds=30):
        self._max_messages = max_messages
        self._max_seconds = max_seconds
        self._accepting_messages = True
        self._messages = []

        # Fire off a timer to commit when time elapses.
        self._countdown_thread = threading.Thread(target=self.countdown)
        self._countdown_thread.start()

    @property
    def accepting_messages(self):
        return self._accepting_messages

    def countdown(self):
        time.sleep(self._max_seconds)
        self._commit()

    def commit(self):
        threading.Thread(target=self._commit).start()

    def _commit(self):
        self._accepting_messages = False
        // do_something_with(self._messages)
        for message in self._messages:
            message.ack()

    def add_message(self, message):
        self._messages.append(message)
        if len(self._messages) >= self._max_messages:
            self._commit()
```

And then, your callback becomes something like:

```
lock = threading.Lock()
batch = None

def my_callback(message):
    with lock:
        if batch is None or not batch.accepting_messages:
            batch = Batch()
        batch.add_message(message)
```

A few thoughts:

    * Refer to [our publisher batch](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/tree/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/publisher/batch) for an implementation of an extremely similar concept. We were worried about pluggability so what we did is more complex than what you need, but the basic principles all remain the same.
    * Obviously do not rely on the scoping I did in my callback block. :-)

@magoarcano : See @pcostell's note from above, specifically https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/datastore-ndb-python/blob/master/demo/task_list.py which is a demo app using NDB that runs inside GCE. Pay special attention to the configuration settings that turn things off.

Also, As Patrick says, the performance of this will be sub-par due to synchronous requests and no caching.

It's important to note that this is an incredibly complex undertaking because the run time environments are significantly different and NDB was built on the premise that it would only ever run in App Engine (which is an "all or nothing" service with Datastore, Memcache, Task Queues, etc all available via RPC calls). In Flex (or GCE, or AWS, etc) things are very different so a majority of those assumptions don't hold anymore.

This means that even though a port of NDB might work, there will be a huge number of "Oh, we didn't realize NDB made *that* assumption!" moments, so we're being extra cautious about what we release to the world. We don't want to hand out code that works only in the right circumstances -- it should work everywhere.

`multiprocessing`:

  * `close`: puts `None` on task `Queue` thus signaling "end of queue"
  * `join`: calls `join` on the `task_handler`, `results_handler`, and all `Process` instances in the pool
  * `terminate` calls `close`, then `terminate` on all `Process` instances in the pool, then `join`

Note that pool is just a bunch of `Process` instances with the same input and output queues.
```
        self._pool = [
            Process(target=worker, args=(self._inqueue, self._outqueue,
                                         initializer, initargs))
            for i in range(processes)
            ]
```
And the results and task handlers are just threads that share the above queues.
```
        self._task_handler = threading.Thread(
            target=Pool._handleTasks,
            args=(self._taskqueue, self._inqueue, self._outqueue, self._pool)
            )
        self._task_handler.setDaemon(True)
        self._task_handler._state = RUN
        self._task_handler.start()

        self._result_handler = threading.Thread(
            target=Pool._handleResults,
            args=(self._outqueue, self._cache)
            )
        self._result_handler.setDaemon(True)
        self._result_handler._state = RUN
        self._result_handler.start()
```

Note: good to remember these are `__init__` for `Process` (esp. identity):
```
        self._identity = _current_process._identity + (counter,)
        self._authkey = _current_process._authkey

## 中共的流氓本性

「中國特色」是中共的遮羞布。中共一向宣稱它在中國革命中的成功要歸功於「馬克思列寧主義同中國革命的具體實踐相結合」。濫用「特殊性」是中共的一貫手法，為其反覆無常的流氓政策作了理論舖墊。

（一）反覆無常，瞞天過海

在這種流氓性的「中國特色」招牌下，中共成就的卻只有荒謬和可笑。

共產黨革命的目的是實現生產資料公有制，欺騙了許多年輕人為了共產大同的理想加入黨的隊伍，其中有不少人背叛了有資產的家庭。但83年之後，資產階級又回來了，只不過這次變成了當初打著「大同」旗幟的共產黨自己。如今，在中國共產黨領導人的子女、親屬中，不乏腰纏萬貫的新資本家。不少共產黨員們也爭先恐後地加入這個隊伍。共產黨以革命的名義消滅了地主和資本家，掠奪了他們的私有財產，現在黨的新貴們貪污腐敗，成了比過去資本家還更富的官僚資本暴發戶。對於那些跟隨黨打天下的人來說，真是「早知如此，何必當初」。流血奮鬥了幾十年，到頭來看，只不過是把自己父兄的財產和自己的一生奉獻給了共產黨這個邪教。

共產黨講經濟基礎決定上層建築，現實中黨的貪官污吏的官僚經濟基礎決定著上層高壓建築，所以鎮壓人民成為黨的基本路線政策。

中共又一個流氓特性就是可以給人類文化中的任何概念更換內涵，然後用這些變異概念去批判和專政所有的人。比如黨，其實人類社會中結黨的現象古來都有，遍佈中外。但是只有共產黨完全超出了黨集團的利益範疇。你入了它那個黨，它就要控制你一切乃至人性、生存和私生活。你讓它當權，它就要控制社會、政府、國家機器的一切。大到誰做國家主席，誰做國防部長，制定甚麼法規條例，小到誰能在哪裏居住，和誰結婚，生幾個孩子，而且把這些控制方法發展到無以復加的地步。

中共打著辯證法的名義，徹底破壞哲學的圓融思維方式、思辯能力和探索精神。共產黨講的是「按勞分配」，「讓一部份人先富起來」的過程完成的卻是「按權分配」。打著「全心全意為人民服務」的幌子，欺騙有此類美好理想的人，然後對這些人進行洗腦和全面控制，逐漸把這些人變成「全心全意為黨服務」而不敢為民請命的馴服工具。

（二）「中國特色」的流氓黨

正是這種為了黨的利益不顧一切的原則，中共以邪教的運作方式扭曲了中國社會，在人類社會造出了一個真正的另類。這個另類和任何國家、政黨、團體都不一樣。它的原則是無原則，在它的微笑背後沒有誠意可言。不過善良的人們理解不了中共，因為他們按人類通行的道德標準推測中共，無法想像竟有那麼流氓的一個東西，代表著一個國家。黨以這樣的「中國特色」躋身於世界民族之林。「中國特色」是「中共流氓特色」的縮寫。

中國跛足資本主義就變成了「中國特色的社會主義」；「失業」變成了中國特色的「待業」；「解僱」變成了中國特色的「下崗」；「貧窮」變成了中國特色的「社會主義初級階段」；言論、信仰自由的「人權」變成了中國特色的「生存權」。

（三）國家流氓化，中華民族面臨空前的道德危機

九十年代初期，中國開始流行一句話──「我是流氓我怕誰」，這就是中共幾十年流氓治國的惡果──國家流氓化。伴隨著中國經濟的虛假繁榮，是整個社會道德的全面下滑。

中國人大代表在開會期間大談「誠信」問題，中國高考作文要寫「誠信」題目，可見「喪失誠信」、「道德問題」已經成為中國社會的一場看不見卻又無處不在的巨大危機。貪污腐敗，假貨氾濫，欺詐成風，人心卑劣，世風急下，人與人之間沒有了基本的信任。

口口聲聲滿足於生活改善了的人們，最關心的不就是生活的穩定嗎？甚麼是社會穩定的最重要因素？就是道德。一個道德淪落的社會是不可能有安全保障的。

時至今日，中共幾乎已經鎮壓了所有的傳統宗教，解體了傳統的價值觀，而中共對財富不擇手段地攫取，對人民不擇手段地欺騙，上樑不正下樑歪，迅速帶動全社會走向流氓化。靠流氓手段執政的中共，從本質上講也正需要一個流氓社會作為其生存的環境，因此中共想方設法要把人民拉下水，試圖把中國人民變成程度不同的大大小小的各種流氓。中共的流氓本性就是在這樣葬送著維繫中華民族的道德根基。

結語

「江山易改、本性難移」。歷史證明，中共每次放鬆枷鎖和鐵鏈，都並不意味著會放棄鎖鏈。上世紀60年代初期的大饑荒之後，中共曾經以「三自一包」來恢復農村生產，但那並不意味著中共會改變中國農民「農奴」的地位。八十年代的「自由化」和「經濟改革」，也絲毫不影響1989年中共對人民舉起屠刀。未來，中共也絕不會因為改換了門面，而改變其流氓本性。

如果覺得已經事過境遷，此黨已非彼黨，而滿足於眼前的假象，甚至誤認為共產黨已經改良，或者正在改良，或者有意改良，從而不斷地淡忘過去，那就是給予中共流氓集團繼續生存為害人類的機會。

共產黨所有的努力，就是要人們「忘記」；而人民所有的掙扎，則是要努力「記住」。

事實上，共產黨的歷史，就是一部割裂人民記憶的歷史，是後一代不知道前一代真相的歷史，是億萬百姓生活在對共產黨過去的咒罵、與對共產黨現實的期望這種巨大矛盾之中經歷磨難的歷史。

當共產主義這個邪靈來到人世間，共產黨通過流氓起義和痞子革命奪取政權在人間立足後，它幹的就是通過血腥暴政來建立和維護一個「黨附體」形式的專制社會。以反自然、反天理、反人性、反宇宙的所謂「鬥爭」精神，來摧毀人類的良知善念，來摧毀人類的傳統文明和道德觀念，用血腥屠殺和強制洗腦來製造一個全民瘋狂的共產邪教的一統天下。在共產黨的歷史上，有過紅色恐怖到達頂峰的猖狂時期，也有過幾近滅亡、落荒而逃的狼狽時刻，共產黨每每都是靠了耍盡流氓來度過危機走向下一個猖狂，繼續愚弄人民百姓。

當人們都能認識到共產黨的流氓本性，並不為其假象所矇蔽的時候，也就是終結中共及其流氓本性的時候。

pex adheres to what's in the standard lib and in PEPs.  My reluctance with this feature is that we're going out of the bounds of the python spec and more into the realm of cx_Freeze and p2app, which is a slippery slope of elaborate hacks to make stuff work when we already have ubiquitous solutions for managing dependencies holistically: docker, appc, etc.

It feels like this feature could be a wrapper around pex rather than baked into pex itself, where the wrapper is either heavyweight (docker) or lightweight (walk the pex, introspect the necessary .dylib/.so via something like pyelftools, and construct a shar or zip that sets up the path and execve's the pex.)

Hola @ulisesmarx,

1. El método en Python de `conekta.Charge.create` aún seguirá en las siguientes versiones o estará como deprecated? 

  El flujo de cargos está siendo deprecado y para aprovechar OxxoPay tendrán que ocupar la nueva versión. Estaremos haciendo unos cambios en las proximas 2 semanas para simplificar la lógica de re-intentos, pero cualquier comentario al respecto avisanos para tomarla en consideración.

2.  Por qué el atributo de `unit_price` es de tipo [String, Integer]? Si quiero manejar decimales en este caso es confuso tener que usar 100050 para representar "100.50"

  Independiente de si mandas String o Integer el valor deberia de representar el monto en centavos, te va arrojar un error de validación "100.50" en la nueva versión de hecho.  Ahorita tenemos pendiente lanzar un cambio para hacer este mensaje de error más claro. 
 
3.  Hasta que versión la API de PHP descartará el objeto de "card" directamente en el cargo.

  Igual como el punto unos, el objecto card tal cual está depcrecado y no existe en la nueva versión, sino payment_source (para en el futuro poder aceptar otros tipos de pago). Si ya tienes cards en Conekta, simplemente será el payment_source en la proxima versión.  Cualquier comentario o duda está bien bienvenido.

Saludos,

This can also play tricks when testing locally and switching between versions. Assuming that tox runs isolated environments between switching branches is wrong, because `*.pyc` files remain when checking out different branches.

Because Tox links the git structure into the tox environment, all the `.pyc` files from previous runtimes are included, for instance migrations and contents of `kolibri/dist`. These files are used, even though they are orphans.

Moreover, the local `kolibri/dist` is overwritten when running `tox` because of the linking - not the one in the tox environment.

It is a good point. The problem is that there is not a clean way to discover which parameters should be transformed. I see two different approaches to handle this case:

1) Keep a list of relevant parameters in EmbeddingComposite and transform them appropriately.

Pro:
* simplest implementation

Con:
* maintainability
* the abstraction is far less clean

2) Propagate the list of parameters that need to be transformed up from `DWaveSampler`. I could imagine making that a property of Structured samplers.

Pro:
* keeps the abstraction

Con:
* we would need to create a set of rules for indicating how input parameters get transformed
* maintainability is still an issue, though less than in option (1)

The problem is that `arrays_to_variables` doesn't have enough information to do its job when data types get complex.  Particularly, different decisions need to be made around batching with different data structures, and the information you need to make those decisions was lost by the time we got to `arrays_to_variables`.

Mark, Joel and I discussed the options here yesterday, and this seemed like the best solution.  This removes `arrays_to_variables` entirely and just creates variables to start with.

Pros:
- The API actually now works for complex data types, where the previous solution was fundamentally untenable.
- No more overhead of first creating numpy arrays then converting them to pytorch variables.
- Removing an intermediate API call makes the API simpler.

Cons:
- We now return pytorch `Variables` instead of numpy arrays, so it's harder to use this outside of pytorch.  This isn't actually that big of a deal, because you can get back numpy arrays by just calling `.data.cpu().numpy()`.  Also, while interoperability is nice, and trying to be sure the data code was interoperable led to some nice API decisions early on, it's not a high priority.  In particular, we prefer an API that actually does what over an API that's natively compatible with systems that probably very few people will actually use it for.

Notably, the single vs. multi GPU situation isn't changed by this PR.  It was hard before, and it's still hard, and this doesn't really change anything there, it just moves the lines of code that would have had to be changed to somewhere else.

I also wasn't too thrilled with the `NotImplementedError` methods.  I missed the call to the superclass constructor, but it's the same problem.  I was considering suggesting making `Dataset` an abstract API, with concrete `InMemoryDataset` and `LazyDataset` classes, where `InMemoryDataset` defines the methods that `LazyDataset` doesn't need.  It seemed a little overboard to me, though.

The trouble with passing in the vocab to the `Dataset` is that the `DatasetReader` (which builds the `Dataset`) can't have access to it - it hasn't been built yet.  You could maybe get around this by having the `DataIterator` handle indexing, as you suggest.

I think the fundamental difference between lazy and non-lazy datasets, in terms of the computation done, is that non-lazy datasets can index all of the data once and store the result in memory, saving computation when iterating multiple times over the data.  In fact, that's probably the _reason_ that we need to be lazy in the first place - the text representing the data probably doesn't take all that much memory, but the indexed tensors do.  Everything else is largely the same - lazy datasets will (probably) read from disk while they're iterating over the data, so it'll be slower, but the basic algorithm for going over the data is the same.  

So, this is a long way of saying that the difference in behavior in `index_instances` (and the fact that `iterinstances()` does indexing in the `LazyDataset`) is kind of the _definition_ of the difference between lazy and non-lazy datasets.  I think the right thing to do here is to update documentation for what these methods do, instead of trying to make them behave the same.  `index_instances` could be defined as "Makes it so that instances returned when iterating over this dataset have already been indexed using the given vocabulary.  _When_ the indexing computation happens is determined by the concrete `Dataset` class."

The only problem I see is in relation to the collector processes. The main process will respawn a collector if it sees that it's no longer alive (~every 1 second). This behaviour was added by commit 2025384. Therefore, I'm not sure it'll be possible for collectors to gracefully terminate themselves, since the main process will respawn it when it detects its no longer running. This can get a little messy since I don't think the control-group KillMode can guarantee sending signals in an ordered fashion, since ideally we'd like the main process to die first, so that it doesn't get the chance to respawn a collector. Essentially, we'd be adding a new race condition.

Therefore, unless we remove the 're-load collector if it crashes' behaviour, I'm not sure this is the best approach. IMO, to fix this specific issue, we need the service file to set KillMode to 'process'. That way, combined with fd4146d, all diamond processes will be gracefully shutdown:

i) SIGTERM will be sent to the main process. 
ii) collectors and handlers will then be terminated within for loop in shutdown_handler()
iii) main process will exit
iv) SyncManager will then exit.

I think a separate issue can be created for tidying up signal handlers.

What are your thoughts on this?

Honestly, I do a dirty, filthy monkey patch of the `apicontrollers` module:

```python
import logging
from authorizenet import apicontractsv1, apicontrollers

log = logging.getLogger('creditcard')

if not settings.AUTHORIZE_NET_LOG:
    log.setLevel(logging.CRITICAL)

def _fake_config (*args, **kwargs): # pragma: no cover
    pass
log.basicConfig = _fake_config
apicontrollers.logging = log
apicontrollers.apicontrollersbase.logging = log
```

`apicontrollersbase` sets up a logger, and calls `.basicConfig()` on it to set its level and output file, so I monkey-patch that with an empty function, and set my real logging config beforehand. (The `settings` listed above happens to be a Django settings object, so pick your conditional to taste.)

Side note: by importing `apicontrollers` as a module instead of importing `*` from it, that A) prevents `logging` from getting clobbered in the namespace, and B) allows only monkey-patching `apicontrollers.logging`, leaving you free to log what you actually want to.

Karl found that a global install fails on Ubuntu 14.04 because the Debian package `python-six` ships with v1.5.6
That package is depended upon by a bunch of other Ubuntu packages, and it's all a sticky ball of mud.

Can we consider what it would be like if we wrote with `six>=1.4.1,<2.0.0`?
I know we'd have to handle the ConfigParser location change ourselves, but I think that's it.

I'm also okay with just putting six in tree (in the SDK), since it has no dependencies. However, I think that's actually significantly more work than just expanding the set of versions we support.
We already cast a pretty wide net on `requests` because people are likely to have that installed and we don't need any advanced features, so this would be "consistent" with that, FWIW.

The second thoughts of this PR:

**[Previously]** 
* The transaction and receipt roots are calculated in `State.add_transaction()`.
* Each time `VM.apply_transaction()` is called, the `vm.block.header` **is updated with the latest `transaction_root` and `receipt_root`**.

**[Now]**
* `State.add_transaction()` returns `receipt`.
* The transaction and receipt roots are calculated in `VM.import_block(self, block)`. 
* Each time `VM.apply_transaction()` is called, the `vm.block.header` **isn't** updated with the latest `transaction_root` and `receipt_root`.

The fastest way to solve it may be calculating the roots in `vm.mine_block()` instead of in `vm.import_block()`, and we lost the propoerty of "having `vm.block` always has the latest roots info".

I'm sorry you're having trouble. I have a radical suggestion for both of you: try _removing anaconda_. Not the entire distribution, mind you, just the Anaconda metapackage. In other words:
```
conda remove anaconda --force
```
The reason this helps is that the various versions of Anaconda select _exact_ versions and builds of the various python packages. This has obvious benefits most of the time, but once the packages are _installed_, the `anaconda` metapackage prevents those packages from being updated separately.

Once you've removed `anaconda`, now try doing `conda update --all`, or any other operation you need to. With that metapackage out of the picture, the solver should have an easier time resolving dependency conflicts.

Please let me know what you come up with! In the meanwhile I'm going to investigate alternate solutions.

You said:

>>  I've uninstalled and reinstalled entire conda.

then:

> Above I said I've reinstalled it

I believe you reinstalled Miniconda and added a new user because you believed that would reset your operating system back to a completely clean slate? This is not the case, nothing short of reinstalling you OS will do that.

I think you are using Powershell here considering how you've printed out your `PATH`, but I'm not sure, even though I asked you explicitly:

> How do you launch scipy? Do you use the `Anaconda Prompt`?

We do not currently support `Powershell`.

In your `PATH` I see no mention of any entries from the `Anaconda Distribution` which leads me to believe you have not activated your environment correctly (or at all). Even once you do these things you may still have trouble, but it will be a step in the right direction.

So, the next steps for getting to the bottom of this are:

1. Use `The Anaconda Prompt` and not `Powershell`. If you don't have `The Anaconda Prompt` then activate your env correctly from `cmd.exe` by running `C:\Users\Ertugrul.Karademir\Miniconda3\Scripts\activate`, then try `scipy`.

2. If that fails, then do the same, but before running the `activate` batch file, set your `PATH` to a minimal one to get rid of all those potentially harmful entries via `set PATH=%SystemRoot%\system32;%SystemRoot%;%SystemRoot%\System32\Wbem;%SYSTEMROOT%\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\`

3. If that fails then ensure you have neither `PYTHONHOME` nor `PYTHONPATH` env vars set and try again.

4. If that fails, use something like `procmon` to try to figure out which DLLs that should be getting found in your `C:\Users\Ertugrul.Karademir\Miniconda3` are instead getting found in `C:\Windows\System32`.

I will say that this is more work than it needs to be, but the root cause is the design of how Windows looks for DLLs. I have been thinking of ways to make this diagnosis easier, but I don't have time to implement anything at present.

Finally, in the case offered at the top of the issue, we have this:
```
The following packages will be SUPERCEDED by a higher-priority channel:

    cudatoolkit:   7.5-0             defaults --> 7.0-1             numba
    llvmdev:       3.7.1-4           numba    --> 3.7.1-2           antoine
    llvmlite:      0.11.0-py35_0     numba    --> 0.8.0.dev-py35_11 antoine
    pip:           8.1.1-py35_1      defaults --> 0.0.1-py35_0      antoine
    python:        3.5.1-0           defaults --> 3.5.0-4_dbg       antoine  [pydbg]
```
(you'll note I tweaked the language further).

So why did it select the debug build? Well, the first priority conda has is to obtain the latest version and build of the packages on the command line. That's `3.5.0-4` in the case of Python. _Then_ it determines the minimum number of features required to support that package. Unfortunately, you have _only_ a debug version in your channel, so it has no choice but to select it.

What would be the solution here? Well, turning off `channel_priority` is one. Hosting a non-debug build of Python for every debug build that you have is another.

Thanks but the whole point of using docker is to be machine independent. It
is not great if you have to check different machine capabilities each time
you rebuild the image. My quick fix is to downgrade to version 1.0.0 which
does not have machine dependencies. However this is not ideal as there may
be bugs that have since been fixed.

I am sure I am not the only one using docker and this was a tricky issue to
diagnose because it only happens on on certain machines. I am launching
machines with the lowest spot price at the time. So it seems everything is
tested and working then AWS change their prices and it fails with "invalid
instruction" errors. Most packages do not have hardware specific
compilation in setup.py as it causes unpredictable behaviour.

Ideally I would suggest making it hardware independent. If this does not
work then perhaps have a bcolz_portable version on pypi that is hardware
independent. Looks like that would be fairly easy to include in a build
script?

@tivaliy I would not put the source files inside the `sigasi_project` folder since it is a generated folder. The sigasi project is never checked into version control but is just an artifact that is generated by a script.

I do not know why you cannot use modelsim as an external compiler in Sigasi when you have used SigasiProjectCreator to create the project. I myself never use an external compiler in Sigasi since i use VUnit to compile and run my simulations.

Setting a single "top level" in sigasi and using it to launch a simulation makes no sense when having 10-100 of test benches which each can be a top level for simulation like you can do with VUnit. VUnit also supports launching multiple runs of the same test bench top level with different combinations of generics.

A project structure I typically use is:
```
project_name/
|-- run.py
|-- sigasi_project # This folder is generated by a script and not checked into version control
|-- src
     |--- module1
            |---- file.vhd
            |---- tb_file.vhd
     |--- module2
```

`module1` would be compiled into `module1_lib` to get some name entity name conflict protection between modules. My run.py script just uses `glob` to automatically add all modules and files in the `src` folder.

Our script stopped working after the latest "azure-cli" release as well. I suspect that v2.0.8 introduced a backward-incompatible change to the ```--parameters``` argument:
- using 2.0.7 we called ```az group deployment create ... --parameters @/tmp/myparamfile```
- after 2.0.8 we had to change it to ```az group deployment create ... --parameters /tmp/myparamfile``` (notice the lack of ```@``` symbol)

The CLI hint doesn't really explain the difference between a "parameters file" and "raw JSON ... loaded using ```@<file path>``` syntax" (I guess the former should conform to ```https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2015-01-01/deploymentParameters.json#``` and the latter is just a "flat" dict), so I'm not sure if it was a deliberate change to make the CLI more consistent.

The repro does work and this is a bug, but the guilty code here actually has nothing to do with list_blobs. If you actually look at the request being sent for the blob creation the library isn't including the '?' in the blob name. list_blobs just reveals that problem.

The code causing a problem here is the _update_request_uri_query method in _common_serialization. We use the '?' to delimit the query parameter section of the URL and do not appropriately differentiate if it was passed in intentionally as part of the blob name. The functionality right now would be if you did a blob name something like hello?foo=bla, we'd send hello as the blob name and foo=bla as a query param in the url. This isn't really useful and probably wasn't intentional so we should fix. However, as the request path we send to this method frequently does include query parameters (see create_container code for an example) this isn't a trivial change and may take some time.

If I understand the question correctly, you're looking to have abbreviated source values expanded, such as `S Main St` -> `South Main Street`.  That means you're talking about the expansions mechanism in libpostal that can expand `W` to `West`, `Ave` to `Avenue`, and `PA` to `Pennsylvania`.  It can certainly be a benefit to combinatorically create all possible definitions of the tokenized input.  One of my concerns with libpostal's expansions mechanism is that it can be gratuitously liberal with normalizing.  Take, for instance, the address `553 S Main St, Red Lion, PA 17356`.  Running 

`./libpostal "553 s main st red lion pa 17356"`

results in the following:

```
553 south main saint red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 south main saint red lion pa 17356
553 south main street red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 south main street red lion pa 17356
553 san main saint red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 san main street red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 s main saint red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 s main street red lion pennsylvania 17356
553 s main street red lion pa 17356
```

There's probably a context in which `san main saint` is a valid interpretation of `s main st`, but it's probably not what you're looking for in OA, at least in it's current form.  

Here's another example:

`10 R St Washington DC`

The results are:

```
10 r saint washington district of columbia
10 r saint washington dc
10 r saint washington 600
10 r street washington district of columbia
10 r street washington dc
10 r street washington 600
10 river saint washington district of columbia
10 river street washington district of columbia
10 river street washington dc
10 river street washington 600
```

As you can see it expands `R` to `River`, `St` to `Street`, and `DC` to `600` (Roman numerals).  Only 2 of these results are actually correct interpretations of the input (as interpretable by humans).  This example is a bit extreme since OA's usage of it would be only used on the street field, I'm just demonstrating some of the features I've seen.

First off, thanks for the issue :)

So I thought I had addressed this somewhere else before but I couldn't find it -- it's possible I'm remembering a private discussion about the same thing.  I'll try and reiterate my thoughts on that here and we can go from there!

One design goal of a lot of unix utilities is "silence is golden" and for pre-commit the meaning takes a bit of a different form.  As pre-commit is intended to be ~somewhat of a gateway for vetting changes it should generally be as quiet as possible unless something goes wrong.  In other words, attempt to keep as high of a signal-to-noise ratio as possible.  This is also suggested in the design of hooks that plug into the framework ([an example](https://github.com/tox-dev/tox/pull/708#issuecomment-350567303)) -- by default they provide just as much information to answer why they're stopping the commit at all (preferring "filename.py: import reordered" over a full-on diff of a file).

The previous ~similar systems that `pre-commit` replaced had a huge verbosity problem in that _most_ of their output was "warnings".  Yes the messages were probably useful if you were going to take the time to read them, but for 99% of developers if it wasn't stopping them from committing they did not care and ignored the messages.  Not only did this decrease the likelihood that these developers would fix the problems presented in warnings, but it also hurt the attentiveness to messages in other (related **_and_** unrelated) commandline tools.  I'm probably stealing the name from somewhere but I usually refer to this problem as _warning fatigue_.  More succinctly, if users are presented with lots of non-critical information, they are trained to ignore all of the (important) output.

I think you'll find that while you may read the warning messages about code complexity and such, other contributors will filter this out -- and worse may filter out _all_ of pre-commit's messaging.

All that said, implementing such a thing is trivial (though I personally wouldn't use it and I would generally discourage anyone from using it ;) ).

[here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/98ec74dcabd8279f99b072e4232c5042ce16924b/pre_commit/commands/run.py#L133)

```diff
-    if (stdout or stderr or file_modifications) and (retcode or args.verbose):
+    if (
+            (stdout or stderr or file_modifications) and
+            (retcode or args.verbose or hook['verbose'])
+    ):
```

(plus the changes to validating + defaulting this in `pre_commit/clientlib.py`, plus a quick regression test in tests/commands/run_test.py)

Let me know what you think!

Well, the problem is a bit tricky.
Question is: how to process semicolon chars in query part?
This [StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3481664/semicolon-as-url-query-separator) is a good summary, especially the first answer.

Long story short: `;` char was used as a parameter separator for ages along with `&`. 
Python internally process both by `urllib.parse.parse_qsl` function.

Later in 2014 W3C recommendations `;` has gone, only `&` is supported. But `yarl` uses `parse_qsl` -- and works as expected (at least by me).

`aiohttp` cannot just append `params` to the end of url -- it should parse URL and extend it's query part with `params`. That's why we have query part transformation.

Sure, I could don't split query by `;` but not sure what existing code we will break -- looks like people still use old agreements.

Right now there are two workarounds: 

1. Don't use `params` at all and construct full URL with query parameters before passing it to `client.get()`.
2. Percent-encode `;` character (replace it with `'%3B'`) before sending as `params`.

After the CONNECT query to proxy and establish connection to desctination server, proxy became a tunnel.
According to [RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-2.3):
```
A "tunnel" acts as a blind relay between two connections without
   changing the messages.  Once active, a tunnel is not considered a
   party to the HTTP communication, though the tunnel might have been
   initiated by an HTTP request.
```
This can be meant as direct connection

The flow is Ctrl+f to find a word, followed by pressing Escape twice, then you have the cell selected. I can also confirm that notebook 5.4.1 still doesn't allow it.

The reason is jupyter_core uses `execvp()` to launch subcommands. Replacing the call with `subprocess.call()`.

This makes `jupyter nbconvert` annyoing and `jupyter console` unusable under Windows.

IIRC this is a browser limitation, you cannot trigger a window closing too long after an user action, which often need to be a click, or someone else. The definition of "Too long" is variable, and I would expect we do too many indirection and the request to close is cancelled.

There is an issue with `create_wallet()`.

In `python3-indy==1.4.0` it has the following structure: 

`async def create_wallet(pool_name: str, name: str,
xtype: Optional[str], config: Optional[str], credentials: Optional[str]) -> None:`

In particular, there is an issue with `credentials`.

`:param credentials: (optional) Wallet credentials json. List of supported keys are defined by wallet type.
     if NULL, then default config will be used.`

If we don't pass `credentials` here (in other words, if we pass `None` as it was before), then the following line: `c_credentials = c_char_p(credentials.encode('utf-8')) if credentials is not None else None` will pass `None` to Rust.

However, Rust version [checks](https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-sdk/blob/1f59c75b5423ed0bf83a30e6cde6a58aed1ffc46/libindy/src/api/wallet.rs#L183) if passed `credentials` is of type string and raises an Error.

Therefore, among other things, I added in this how-to: 
`wallet_credentials = json.dumps({"key": "wallet_key"})` which is passed later on as `credentials`.

So, I just released a new version that adds support for DRF's built-in pagination classes.  The new version is up on PyPi, so you should be able to update the package to get pagination working.  The info below is from the new README, but I'm putting it here for your reference:

If (and only if) `flat = True` on your view, **drf-multiple-model** supports some of Django Rest Framework's built-in pagination classes, including `PageNumberPagination` and `LimitOffsetPagination`.  Implementatation might look like this:

```
from rest_framework import pagination

class BasicPagination(pagination.PageNumberPagination):
    page_size = 5
    page_size_query_param = 'page_size'
    max_page_size = 10 

class PageNumberPaginationView(MultipleModelAPIView):
    queryList = ((Play.objects.all(),PlaySerializer),
                 (Poem.objects.filter(style="Sonnet"),PoemSerializer))
    flat = True
    pagination_class = BasicPagination
```

which would return:

```
{
    'count': 6,
    'next': 'http://yourserver/yourUrl/?page=2',
    'previous': None,
    'results': 
        [
            {'genre': 'Comedy', 'title': "A Midsummer Night's Dream", 'pages': 350},
            {'genre': 'Tragedy', 'title': "Romeo and Juliet", 'pages': 300},
            {'genre': 'Comedy', 'title': "The Tempest", 'pages': 250},
            {'title': 'Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?', 'stanzas': 1},
            {'title': 'As a decrepit father takes delight', 'stanzas': 1}
        ]
}
```
------------------

I forgot to mention in the README, I also added a `paginate_queryList` function.  Right now this just calls the built in `paginate_queryset`, but it's there to override if you want to create some custom pagination.  Both `PageNumberPagination` and `LimitOffsetPagination` have been tested, but let me know if you have any questions/problems!

Let me see if I can take an initial crack here:

## Hardware and OS requirements

* A 64-bit CPU running at at least 1 GHz is *highly* recommended (but not strictly required)
* You will need ~250MB RAM and ~5 GB disk free.  Do **not** attempt to use a network-attached disk for this.
* You should have at least 15,000 inodes free in your filesystem.  Unless you are using a very small VM image, you almost certainly have enough (you can check with `df -i`).
* TCP port 6264 should be open and support bidirectional traffic.  If you want to use SSL, then port 6263 should be open.
* A reliable Internet connection of DSL-like quality or higher

## Deployment
### The Easy Way
* Install from `pip`, source code, or Docker
* Run `blockstack fast_sync`
* Run `blockstack start`

### The Less Easy Way
* Install from `pip`, source code, or Docker
* Run `blockstack-core start`
* Wait a few days

#### Best Practices for the Less Easy Way
* Take a `blockstack-server.snapshots` database from a known-good node and pass `--expected_snapshots=/path/to/blockstack-server.snapshots`.  This will force your bootstrapping node to verify that it reaches the same sequence of consensus hashes as it bootstraps (i.e. your node will detect any divergence from Blockstack's name history and abort early, instead of wasting your time).
* Make sure you're in a position to leave the node online at 100% CPU use for the duration of its bootstrapping period

### The Hard Way
* Install `bitcoind` (version 0.16.x is recommended for now)
* Start `bitcoind` as `bitcoind -daemon -txindex=1`
* Wait for `bitcoind` to download the entire blockchain.  This can take between 1 hour and 1 day.
* Install `blockstack-core` from source, `pip`, or Docker
* Run `blockstack-core configure` and enter your `bitcoind` node's IP address, port, RPC username, and RPC password when prompted
* Run `blockstack-core start`
* Wait a few days

#### Best Practices for the Hard Way
* You're going to need ~400 GB of space for the Bitcoin blockchain state
* You can safely store its chain state on a network-attached disk, if you're doing this in a cloud-hosted environment
* Your `bitcoind` host will need TCP:8332-8333 open for bidirectional traffic

## Troubleshooting
### The node stops responding to TCP:6264
* Check `dmesg` for TCP SYN flooding.  The solution here is to kill and restart the node.
* To mitigate, install a rate-limiting proxy HTTP server in front of the node.  We have a sample config for `nginx` [here](https://github.com/blockstack/atlas/blob/master/public_fleet/node/default).

### No other Blockstack nodes contact my node
* Verify that your IP address is publicly-routable, and that peers can communicate on TCP:6264

### People are attacking my Bitcoin node
* Stick an `nginx` reverse proxy in front of your `bitcoind` node, and use our [nginx](https://github.com/blockstack/atlas/tree/master/public_fleet/bitcoind) scripts to limit APi access to only the JSON-RPC methods Blockstack actually needs.  Better yet, do what we do---build a statically-linked `bitcoind` binary from source that simply omits all of the RPC methods except the ones listed in the linked config file.

@leesunfreshing Hi, we don't have anything seq2seq specific in Sonnet. The RNN cores we have should be compatible with the TF RNN containers (eg tf.nn.dynamic_rnn) so I imagine using a sonnet module will work... As far as I can see only the decoder is provided because you need to be feeding the decoder always with the output of the previous timestep, and there is also the opportunity to do beam search which requires extra code. For the encoder, you simply feed your sequence into a (different) RNN in the standard way (tf.nn.dynamic_rnn), ignore the output sequence and then use the final hidden state as the input to the decoder. I'm not an expert on seq2seq, but I believe this is why they didn't provide any special code for the encoder.

If you have further questions on this I suggest filing an issue on the main TF GitHub.

You most certainly can, the procedure however is not very well defined at the moment.

I'd be happy to properly document it with you in the Wiki if you're up for giving it a go; and to potentially develop a build-script for future compilations.

In a nutshell, these are the steps I took for the current version.

1. Compile using Python 2.7 x64 and Visual Studio 2013 under Windows 7 or 8.
2. *Paste* the result into the installation of a Riverbank distribution; **overwriting** all existing, but keeping all new.
3. Append [`qt.conf`][qtconf] and augment [`__init__.py`][init]

### 2. Overwrite

This is rather unclean, but when asking Riverbank about which binaries to actually include in a distribution, such as those included in [his installers][rb], he essentially told me he figured it out via "trail and error", so I did the same.

### 3. Qt.conf and init.py

These make PyQt self-contained and are required to make Qt usable without manually registering the platform-plugins. It differs from Riverbanks distribution in that it doesn't pollute the Python root directory and allows PyQt5 to co-exist with PyQt4, if needed.

[rb]: http://www.riverbankcomputing.com/software/pyqt/download5
[init]: https://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5/blob/master/PyQt5/__init__.py
[qtconf]: https://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5/blob/master/PyQt5/qt.conf

@ionelmc : OK, there's two things that you're missing.

First, you're using `src` as the `run.source`. While that will work for an editable install, it's not going to work here, because those aren't the files that are actually running. It'll show those file names, but it'll peg the coverage at 0, except for the tests which are the ones that are being run. Instead, you should be using the package name. In your case it's `nameless`. If you do that, you'll see the same problem I'm having.

Second, it's not combining the files the way it should be. Once the combining is done, all the files left in parallel mode, with filenames like `.coverage.Ryans-MacBook-Air.local.77550.764876`, should be gone, or at least overwritten in favor of `.coverage`, which is the file that can be uploaded to codecov.

It's possible that you'd like there to be the option to _not_ combine those parallel files immediately, for instance if you're running multiple tox jobs that will need to be combined with something after all of those jobs. If that's what you prefer, I can add a new flag or something to make that work. Normalizing the paths in that case probably isn't going to work without some more work to allow us to combine the combined files, since the path normalization happens during combining.

However in my case, and I suspect most cases, it's more important to display the coverage the way it's expected than to make files easily combinable, since having it combine between tox envs is going to mean some kind of orchestration outside of tox, and in any case between pytest runs.

This is part of a class of issues we have.  If a RID is specified, and the project is an Exe, then by default we’ll use the latest patch of Microsoft.NETCore.App.  We run into issues when you reference a project that uses the latest patch from one that doesn’t.

Other related issues:
https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/2364
https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/2253

C++/CX is targeted at the (Windows-specific) WinRT, it's not a general language usable with native libraries, so I don't think it makes much sense for .Net Core.

C++/CLI is not specific to WinRT, so it could make more sense; there's already a discussion about it at the CoreCLR repo: https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr/issues/659.

There is a lot of misunderstanding here, and among desktop .Net developers at large. Developers have a vision - wave a magic wand and my WPF code will run everywhere. But this is not thought through at all.

**Of course when they crated a cross-platform .Net runtime, it could not include Windows-specific bits.** This is called .Net Core.

**Of course they are allowing .Net Core in WPF.** It doesn't make sense to maintain multiple versions of .Net. .Net Core is better than the .Net Framework already. They just need to take out the Windows-specific elements and put it in a separate library for use in WPF.

**Of course .Net Core is not going to have GUI components built in, let alone the whole of WPF.** Many of the systems it must run on don't even have GUIs.

**Of course WPF is windows-only.** Any operating system has to have a UI framework. A cross platform framework would be built on top of WPF or UWP.

You can take WPF and emulate it on other platforms. But: 1. It is technically hard. 2. It is only be feasible on desktop platforms, so only allows you to run on 7% more systems (mainly macs). 3. The results would not be great on those systems. This is not a project that deserves serious investment.

### The right approach:

1. Allow .Net code to run on all platforms, desktop and mobile (.Net Core and mono, eventually just .Net Core), and create UIs on each platform (WPF/UWP/Xamarin.Mac/Xamarin.iOS/Xamarin.Android). This is mostly done already in case you had not noticed.
2. Create an abstraction layer for people who want to do the UI code once. This is also mostly done already, by Xamarin.Forms, although desktop support is more recent and so is not at the same level as mobile support.

So if you want cross-platform .Net apps for mobile or desktop, follow Xamarin. If you want to create cross-platform apps for desktop only using platform-independent abstractions, contribute to Xamarin.Forms by using it, testing it, and writing and extending components for it.

I did some testing on this... I was able to reproduce the `mod_rewrite` problem on some systems (but not all... it may depend on minor versions of Augeas 1.2...) by adding the following to an Apache config file:

```
	RewriteEngine On
	RewriteCond %{HTTPS} off
	RewriteRule ^(.+?(this|that+))$ https://%{HTTP_HOST}%{REQUEST_URI}/$1 [L,R,NE]

	RewriteRule "^/?path/([^/]+)/([^/]+)" "/path?$1=$2" [PT]
        RewriteCond "%{QUERY_STRING}" "(.*(?:^|&))mykey=([^&]*)&?(.*)&?$"
	RewriteCond "%2" !=not-so-secret-value 
	RewriteRule "(.*)" - [F]
	RewriteCond "%{QUERY_STRING}" "(.*(?:^|&))mykey=([^&]*)&?(.*)&?$"
	RewriteRule "(.*)" "$1/products/%2/?" [PT]
	# Remove mykey=???
	RewriteCond "%{QUERY_STRING}" "(.*(?:^|&))mykey=([^&]*)&?(.*)&?$"
	RewriteRule "(.*)" "$1?%1%3"

	RewriteCond "/var/www/my_blog/%{REQUEST_FILENAME}" !-f
	RewriteCond "/var/www/my_blog/%{REQUEST_FILENAME}" !-d
	RewriteRule "^" "index.php" [PT]
	RedirectMatch "^/$" "http://example.com/about/"
```

However, once #1291 was merged the `mod_rewrite` problem seems to have been solved.

I commented out these two lines:

    if authzr.body.identifier != identifier:
        raise errors.UnexpectedUpdate(authzr)

in the `_authzr_from_response` method.

Probably not the best solution but at least it works.

```
        except BufferError as e:

This is good thinking, but do note that flush() will wait for **all** messages to be flushed (delivered or failed) which is a bit excessive if you just need room for one more message.
You might want to consider adding a timeout to flush(), or calling poll() instead that will return as soon as an event occurs (such as a delivery report) - possibly in a loop until the produce() call makes it through.

Hm... I'm conflicted about this. The `keys` method makes it clearer exactly what you're trying to accomplish, so this kind of an "explicit is better than implicit" thing.

In the video, he states without proof that it's better not to use `keys`, but I really don't think the semantics of dictionary iteration is particularly obvious. If I were new to Python, my first thought would be that iterating over a dictionary would return `dict.items()`, not `dict.keys()`.

In terms of performance, I think it's actually not that important either, because the plan with the parser code is to eventually cache that `min` call (so `.keys()` would never be called more than once in that situation). I also think it's unlikely that `tzical.keys()` will ever be called particularly frequently, and I find `list(dict)` returning `list(dict.keys())`  even *more* strange and opaque than iteration returning `keys` is.

@metalmac `powerline` is a script (or binary compiled from a small C script) which generates used prompt/statusline in many cases, including shell and tmux bindings. If `powerline-daemon` was not launched (or it failed to use daemon’s socket, e.g. because daemon was launched so it will use something non-standard) `powerline` will fall back to `powerline-render` script which does basically the same thing, except that it initializes powerline each time it is called what does not allow to support segments run in background threads or using filesystem watchers (etc, some performance-related things). You are not supposed to call either `powerline` or `powerline-render` directly, shell/tmux/etc bindings call `powerline` by themselves. But `powerline-daemon` is not run by the bindings because it is not always wanted.

While there is no SHA1 checksum for the complete large file, each uploaded part of the file still uses a SHA1 checksum. If a transfer is interrupted while uploading a part, it tries reuploading this part again. From your posted log I can't really tell if the sync finished or was aborted because of the exception. If it aborted you can restart the sync and it will resume the unfinished large files (after checking that the SHA1 hash of all uploaded parts match the local file).

Subject: Re: [Backblaze/B2_Command_Line_Tool] Encrypting files during upload/download (#126)

I put together this proof of concept. Some details are still open for debate:

  a.. Do we use AES-128, AES-256 or allow it to be user selectable? Note, that AES-256 isn't necessarily more secure, just because there is a bigger number. Even Bruce Schneier recommends using AES-128 for new applications (at the moment the code still uses AES-256). 
  b.. Number of PBKDF2HMAC iterations: I've set this to 500k iterations, which takes approximately half a second on my 5 year old laptop. Do we make this option user selectable? Running 500k iterations on low power hardware (Raspberry Pi, etc...) might take several seconds. Note that an iteration count of 1 is sufficient for already cryptographically secure inputs. 
  c.. I've implemented filename encryption using CBC mode with the IV determined by a HMAC of the filename. This might not be the most secure way, but should be good enough for encrypting filenames. Because of the required padding in CBC mode, the exact filename length cannot be deduced from the ciphertext. The encrypted filename can be stored in the metadata and the actual filename should be the hash of the encrypted filename. I'm still asking myself how to handle folders. Do we even expose the folder structure on B2? I think that's most of it for now. I already mentioned that I'm not that familiar with Python, so there might be simpler or more portable ways of doing things.

So the more I'm thinking about this, the more I think we should probably bite the bullet and maintain a custom, vectorized, pure python+numpy Newton/Halley solver for i_from_v() and v_from_i(). I think any scipy implementation is going to be a ways off, and I figure the alternative "gold & slow" bracketing method for the first quadrant can remain wrapped with numpy.vectorize for the time being.

We should consider adding (optionally?) a convergence check based on the residual, perhaps in addition to the usual stopping criterion based on the step size. We could also raise instead of warn on a zero derivative assertion failure. I also think that the function should conveniently determine automatically whether to run Newton vs. Halley depending on if the passed function returns two (f and f' for Newton) or three (f, f', and f'' for Halley) values, and this also allows for more computational reuse/efficiency than the scipy implementation (with separate func, fprime, and fprime2 computations on each iteration).

If we do this, then we should make the solver general, because I bet we will want to try it out on something like the maximum power computations by looking for a zero derivative in P vs. V. It doesn't seem like Bisection is worth the effort, but I could be persuaded otherwise here.

For i_from_v() and v_from_i() solutions, it looks like the first derivatives are never zero, but I haven't looked at global convergence guarantees. Has anyone looked at that or have a divergence example? If we can prove convergence, then we could safely relax the convergence checks for additional speedup.

@rueberger:  You *can* call functions from the global namespace in your config function. So if I understand your case correctly you might just want to do this:
```python
@ex.config
def cfg():
    data = generate_data_config(...)
    train = generate_train_config(...)
    model = generate_model_config(...)
```

Additionally, if the corresponding arguments of your pipeline functions are called `data`, `train`, and `model` respectively,  then you can add them as captured functions and have sacred pass them automatically, like this:

``` python
ex.captured(pipeline_data)
ex.captured(pipeline_train)
ex.captured(pipeline_model)

@ex.automain
def main():
    d = pipeline_data()
    m = pipeline_model()
    t = pipeline_train()
```

Ok, so I think I see what's wrong here...

I think that expected behaviour should look like this:
```python
>>> object.__module__
'__builtin__'
>>> object().__module__
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'object' object has no attribute '__module__'
```

It means that class should have `__module__` attribute, but instance of that class should not.

Going back to `Dependency Injector`, currently it behaves like this:

```python
>>> from dependency_injector import providers
>>>
>>> providers.Configuration.__module__
'dependency_injector.providers'
>>> providers.Configuration('config').__module__
<dependency_injector.providers.Configuration('config.__module__') at 0x103277a70>
```

The only problem I see is that `providers.Configuration('config').__module__` should raise an `AttributeError` for special attributes like `__module__`.

Arrays are defined for all elements in their domain. Since arrays over integers have all integers in their domain some value has to be assigned for arbitrary integers in the domains.

Related questions:

- There isn't much in terms of conveniences for defining arrays other than what you can do with SMT-LIB and defining first-order formulas.
- For intervals: this will be better handled when having lambdas, but you can always add quantified formulas that define a function on a range of values. Z3's current module for quantifier reasoning does not do well with arrays (this will be improved, but not for  a while). You will therefore be better off by defining "result" and other "arrays" as functions and constrain their values using axioms.

Sure, if you want it to be a basic tensor variable, you'd need to set it to an InputLayer instance then, which has that variable (or expression) as its `input_var`.

> But couldn't we get remove the support for hid_init being a tensor variable

The point of this Issue (#11) is to allow all network parameters to be theano expressions, so that would naturally include the case of it being a `T.matrix()`. No wait, actually, it would just allow it to be a `T.vector()`, since it's also a vector when it's a shared variable, and the recurrent layer code shouldn't have to distinguish these cases. So we'll break backwards compatibility (but probably for very very few users). Unless we change the recurrent layer code to do the `T.dot(ones, ...)` expansion depending on `hid_init.ndim` instead of `isinstance(hid_init, ...)` and allow `add_param()` to skip the dimensionality check, or accept multiple allowed shapes. Okay, thinking aloud here :)

> and instead allow hid_init to be a layer?

That would be a good idea then, as it allows `hid_init` to be a matrix (with a separate initial state for each example in a mini-batch) instead of a vector.

I guess it would be nice to have a shortcut for this one:
```
loss = apply_penalty(get_all_params(layer, regularizable=True), l2)
```
This is by far going to be the most common use case, i.e. "add regularization terms for all regularizable parameters in a network". If we can avoid 'exposing' the tagging mechanism here, that would be nice. Something like:

```
loss = penalize_net(layer, l2)
```

but maybe with a different name, I don't know :) Just something that encapsulates the `regularizable=True` and the `get_all_params` call.

> Supporting both will by definition require separate code paths in some cases, won't it?

Yes. For cuDNN, the only difference is where to import the `dnn` module from. We have some other places that need adaptation, though:
```
$ grep -le sandbox */*/*.py
lasagne/layers/corrmm.py
lasagne/layers/cuda_convnet.py
lasagne/layers/dnn.py
lasagne/layers/noise.py
lasagne/layers/special.py
```
We'll need to check in detail if they also just boil down to different `import`s.

> Also, if there are no use cases anymore where `Conv2DDNNLayer` offers an advantage over `Conv2DLayer`, we should probably deprecate it and encourage people to switch to the latter.

Its only use is to enforce a specific implementation for a particular layer. This is something you can't do with Theano flags. We should try to communicate this better; people still use the DNN version because it sounds faster.

> Is it just me or does it seem like things could get a bit complicated for integer targets?

Yes, I thought about this and I guess we'll be fine, but it's good to discuss anyway.

> If however the predicted output is a 3d tensor (bs, f, k) (where f is some extra dimension), then the targets are now an 'integer matrix' of size (bs, f), so we'd have to find a nice and clean way to use that matrix to index the tensor.

The integers would index into the third dimension of the 3d tensor. If we rely on Theano's softmax Ops (which we should, to get the logsoftmax optimization), then we'd reshape the predictions to `(bs*f, k)` and the targets to `(bs*f,)` and everything would work out nicely.

> What if we also have the case where the predicted tensor is of shape (bs, k, f) (i.e., the softmax is now done over the 2nd axis)

Then the targets would still be `(bs, f)`. We would need to dimshuffle the predictions to `(bs, f, k)`, flatten them to `(bs*f, k)` and the targets to `(bs*f,)` and again we'd be fine.

> or if the predicted tensor was of shape (bs, k1, k2) (where the softmax is over both k1 and k2)?

That's the trickiest case. I was thinking the integer targets should be of shape `(bs,)` then, indexing into the flattened `k1*k2`. We can then flatten the predictions to `(bs, k1*k2)` and everything works fine. The alternative would be to have integer targets of `(bs, 2)` which give the position in k1 and the position in k2. Then we'd need to compute the flattened `(bs,)` version from that using the shape information from the predictions tensor. We could even support both, since the target dimensionality allows to distinguish the different cases, but I'd start with only the first option. It may be more common to use one-hot-encoded targets for more exotic cases anyway. I'm not sure how much performance differs between indexing and, err, one-hotting.

Does this make sense to you?

> Wouldn't mind some guidance in the process though.

Sure! :)

> What what about the initilialization of `gamma` and `beta`?

Ah. There was a catch. We want `gamma` and `beta` to be shared across the batch, but not normalize data across the batch. This means the interface is actually very different.

Okay, I think we can forget about the common base class and go with an `InstanceNormLayer`.

Now the question is what to do about layer normalization. Checking their paper again (https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450), they normalize across all but the batch axis (i.e., across all features) and learn a scalar shift and scale. I'm leaning towards omitting this. Layer normalization gives each neuron its own scale and shift, so it's not tied to the normalization axes as in batch normalization. It's probably easier to just use a sequence of instance normalization, bias and scale layers for this.

The unfortunate thing about the reassembler tool is that there's no clear way to create several versions of a binary with _different_ patch sets applied. But it'd be something like `project.tools.reassembler.patch_binary("patched_bin", [ InsertCodePatch(...) ])`... Basically, in that design, a Tool would be an analysis that mostly provides actionable functionality ("generate a patch") rather than retrieval of knowledge ("recover the CFG") and has a sort of "append-only" state (such as `find_gadgets` for angrop).

The slight relation between "append only" and "incremental" is interesting...

> How do you convert the Shimple in python object?

In the same way whereby we convert VEX IR into a Python based one: We create Python classes for each statement and expression class in Soot, and fill in the data we get from a Shimple representation of a program.

> How can be this object be executed and have the same kind of result that you would have executing VEX?

There will be a lot of changes, and I don't think I can detail any of them, as most of the effort in terms of symbolically executing Soot code is nowhere close to done. In brief, we are creating a new environment model for executing Soot code. This environment model will be quite different from the one you currently use in angr when symbolically executing binary code. Eventually, this Java environment should be able to co-exist with the legacy binary code environment, which will allow some interesting analyses.

However, I'm not sure how much effort we are going to spend on it, and when it will be ready. In my previous posts, I was merely saying we will have Java support. But I did not promise anything for symbolic execution on Java code.

Well, the interpretation of HDOP isn't correct throughout that is what I meant, and so I still do not think that it is correct in the documentation even though the GPS_RAW message says the eph is in cm. The GPS_RAW message is just a protocol to pack the raw GPS information, for which the original information is drawn from the NMEA message being sent by the GPS module. And if we look at the NMEA message protocol the HDOP information is unitless. @hamishwillee did mention about some scaling being carried out in a driver. So I am looking into that. 

I do agree with you and Hamish that this is an ArduPilot issue, and so I will go raise this issue on the Ardupilot forum as well. Thanks so much and let me know if any new information comes up regarding this.

pip install dronekit (this added pymavlink back in but not the version I want)

cd mavlink
git clone https://github.com/Ardupilot/pymavlink (by using clone in the mavlink directory the build can find the message definitions and there is no need for extra work)
setup.py build install

Hope it helps.

Hey @dfishburn,

> Problem I have is when CURL sends something to Splash, it uses a GET and the JavaScript is executed and the page is returned correctly. If I issue the same CURL command with -X POST, the JavaScript is not executed (or takes too long) and I only get back HTML.

Scrapy-splash uses [Splash HTTP API](http://splash.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html); POST requests has different format - they must use `application/json` content type and pass arguments in json-encoded body. In Splash they are strictly more powerful than GET because they allow to encode data to arrays/objects, and because there is no a limit on arguments length. Some Splash features are not available when using GET requests.

Note that these POST/GET we're talkling about are HTTP methods used to connect to Splash, not HTTP methods used to send requests to remote websites; the latter is controlled by [http_method](http://splash.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#arg-http-method) argument or by parameters of [splash:go](http://splash.readthedocs.io/en/stable/scripting-ref.html?#splash-go) and alike functions. 

I think GET requests are more suited for quick testing or for embedding Splash results (e.g. screenshots) directly to webpages; to use all features clients should always prefer `application/json` POST requests.

Ok so in that case I'm going to ask you to update this post (preferably by editing your main post) with new minor bugs you find.

This is for two reasons: first, having many small bugs on the issue tracker pollutes the space and makes game-breaking issues less visible for other people searching for problems they are having. Second, every time someone makes an issue it requires us to think: "What is broken? Is this bug a real bug, a mistake by the submitter, or something we are temporarily okay with? What should the correct user experience be? How can this be fixed? Will those fixes impact other pieces of functionality in Cass? How much work is required to implement the correct fix; and related, how important is this fix related to all the other things we have to do, both in Cass and in real life? Finally, how should we respond to the submitter?" Just answering those questions, even before we do any actual work, is non-trivial and takes some time (and you'd be amazed how much time this project consumes, even without us using it for our own projects).

I'd bet there are between 100 and 300 of these bugs in Cass if you really go looking, and you've found a few of them so far. However, since we do this in our spare time, we don't have the time to fix (and figure out how to fix) them all right now (although we hope we can someday). So part of our job is to prioritize the things that affect the functionality that people (especially us!) want to use, and weigh that along with what sounds like fun to do (so we don't get too burned out). For example, testing the new SQLAlchemy store, documenting it, and getting other databases (e.g. Mongo and Reddis) available to users are a few not-super-fun things that imo are very important to do.

These bugs are less important, but together many minor improvements can provide a very significant user improvement. To that end, I've renamed this issue "minor bugs" and I ask that you edit your post in the future with additional small improvements you (and others) identify. We'll check back on this thread regularly when we have some time and energy to see what's new and what can be improved. As the bugs pile up, we'll occasionally make a run through and try to check some of them off the list.

If you find bugs that are significantly impacting your ability to use Cass productively, please create a new issue so that we can prioritize it independently.

I hope that makes sense.

P.S. to anyone else reading this: We really appreciate your help answering questions and issues, it's a big deal, so thank you.

Do you agree that we should decide on what the `__init__` and `featurize` operations takes for inputs (i.e, between those two options in my last message) before we merge this branch?

I'm concerned that having two different patterns for using featurizers will be confusing if they are both used for a single training set. For example, it is not uncommon to use both "composition-based" and "structure-based" features to describe materials, and it would be tedious to have a different usage pattern for each type of feature. 

That said, I'm not sure how best to make the first pattern work for the site-based features. Perhaps `featurize` should take a tuple of (structure, site index)? Regardless, we can figure that out later. What I'm looking to do before we merge is gather consensus on the general pattern for using an `AbstractFeaturizer`. I would argue for adopting the first pattern (i.e., featurizer options to constructor, description of entry to `featurize`), and am interested to get your opinion.

So here is something that worked for me:
1. Do not use Sudo while opening your Python/ IDE, as LIBRARY_LD_PATH is empty for sudo user.
2. Instead always open your Python editor (PyCharm in my case) with ./pycharm.sh command without sudo. 
3. Here is the key, even after updating bashrc, always close all terminals/ IDE and open again before running your IDE again. This should be done as even after updating bashrc, the IDE reads the old bashrc file which does not even exist anymore.

I propose the fix as follows:

* ipware to introduce ipware.ip3.get_client_ip() to fix current issues.
* ipware to not take any parameters other than request. The rest should be configuration. That will make the library more useful. django-axes and other users need not introduce their own configuration parameters just to pass to ipware.
* ipware to default to assuming that the service is not running behind a proxy server as @pope1ni suggested. Assuming direct service by default is secure in all cases. Assuming behind proxy by default in insecure in some cases. This is similar to Django itself.
* ipware to not have precedence order at all. Instead it should only allow specifying a single header to extract client address from. Allowing multiple headers opens users to security problems. It is hard to imagine a use case for accepting two headers securely. Have two proxies that send two different headers? You need to configure each one of them to reset the other header anyway, so why not configure them to send the same header? Falling back to REMOTE_ADDR when configured header value is unexpected may be okay.
* ipware to not assume that ip address count in the value of header will be same as number of proxies. This assumption easily fails when clients send their own header and proxies append to the headers (default for most proxies).
* ipware to thoroughly document all the settings and their security implications.
* (optionally) ipware to implement more thorough handling of trusted proxies. This include consideration for checking REMOTE_ADDR itself in proxy list and performing normalized ipv6 address matching.
* (optionally) ipware to parse the 'Forward' header differently according to [RFC 7239](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7239).
* django-axes to make no changes expect for asking users to look at ipware documentation for configuration in case the project is deployed before proxy servers. Default will be to assume that service is not running behind proxy.

@yurishkuro `__all__` is only used for `from foo import *` style imports, and limits the things being imported when that happens. Even when `__all__` is implemented, you can still import specific things. In specific, it is specified how `__all__` works in the [python style guide pep8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#global-variable-names)

Here is an example:

```
$ cat << EOF > foo.py
> __all__ = ('bar', 'barzz')
> 
> bar = "NOPE"
> 
> def barz():
>     pass
> EOF
$ python
Python 2.7.14 (default, Mar 14 2018, 13:36:31) 
[GCC 7.3.1 20180303 (Red Hat 7.3.1-5)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

>>> from foo import *
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'barzz'
>>> from foo import bar
>>> from foo import barz
>>> barz
<function barz at 0x7f1ee2875320>
>>> bar
'NOPE'
```

```
$ python3.6
Python 3.6.5 (default, Apr  4 2018, 15:01:18) 
[GCC 7.3.1 20180303 (Red Hat 7.3.1-5)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from foo import *
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/code.py", line 91, in runcode
    exec(code, self.locals)
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'foo' has no attribute 'barzz'
>>> from foo import bar, barz
>>> barz
<function barz at 0x7f066db370d0>
>>> bar
'NOPE'
```

I prefer using [isort](https://pypi.org/project/isort/) to automatically manage explicit imports, but this is zero harm in this specific change. It will be fully backwards compatible with anything that imports directly. My only nit with this PR is that `__all__` can be a tuple, and that is generally more efficient (but this is a micro-optimization)

The napalm test framework automatically generates unit tests based on mocked data stored in `test/<driver>/mocked_data/test_<method>/<test name>/`

In this case, you'll need to create `test/nxos/test_ping/normal/`.

If you run tests after that, it will start generating more specific errors:
* First it will complain it can't find file it's trying to load mocked data from.   Create a device and store the output of the specific command in the correct format in the file.
* Then it will complain that it can't load the expected result.  Create the expected_result.json file with what the function SHOULD return, based on the mocked input data.

The problem is that none of the input .xls files are empty.

I've got this whittled down now to eliminate the overhead added to the instance by instead caching the base types for each python type encountered in `registered_types_py` (and using a `weakref` to delete the cache entry if the python type itself is destroyed).

This simplifies a lot: the `all_type_info` struct is gone, replaced with an `all_type_info()` function that returns a vector lvalue reference of base types.

I also rewrote some of the dispatch code to not bother with an `all_type_info` lookup in certain cases—such as an exact type match.  There's still a slight overhead for calling an inherited function, but the overhead now consists solely of the cache lookup (assuming the type has been seen once before); no base type traversal.

Another nice thing here: I combined the `type_caster_generic` and `copyable_holder_caster` logic into a single function.  The duplication of nearly identical logic between the two has bothered me for a while; this eliminates it by templating the implementation function so as to allow a virtual-like dispatch for the few parts that differ without actually using virtual inheritance.  (It does mean each distinct `copyable_holder_caster<T, H>` gets its own implementation, but that was the case before, too: this is all about reducing the code duplication).

This relies on #892 (the weakrefs trigger the failure in the embedding tests).  I've temporarily included that commit in this PR to get the tests to pass.

I've been looking for a way to fix this properly. The main issue is that the C `stdout` redirection fails randomly on Windows. Pytest captures both Python's `stdout` and the real C `stdout` -- only the latter may fail. This can be fixed in one of two ways:

1. Fix the random C `stdout` failure on Windows.
2. Direct all output to Python's `stdout`.

For option 1, I haven't been able to reproduce the bug consistently enough to diagnose or report anywhere. It seems to happen less frequently  when pytest is called directly rather than via cmake target. It also happens less frequently as the number of tests is reduced. This makes it very hard to make a small reproducible case. Based on the debugging I was able to do, it does not seem to be a buffering issue, but rather a redirection issue (output is not in the buffer, it's lost). Pytest seems to do everything correctly, using Python's `os` and `FileIO` API to redirect `stdout` to a file. However, the entire implementation in Python is based on Unix-like file descriptors, which is suboptimal on Windows ([see issue](https://bugs.python.org/issue12939)). The source of [`fileio.c`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.5/Modules/_io/fileio.c#L19-L24) also states:

> Known likely problems:
>  - ...
>  - Making it work on Windows and other oddball platforms

and later [`/* XXX Windows support below is likely incomplete */`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.5/Modules/_io/fileio.c#L879), which does not inspire confidence.

Since I've hit a dead end with option 1, I'm inclined to implement option 2. That would just a matter of introducing a `py::print(Args...)` function and then replace all occurrences of `std::cout << v1 << v2 << std::endl` with `py::print(v1, v2)`. Here, `py::print` would replicate Python's API, so things like `py::print(v1, v2, "sep"_a="-", "end"_a="\n\n")` would also be possible. It may actually be a nice function to have, not just for resolving this issue, but for convenience in general.

I think the issue is coming about because the functions calling the functions with the static local are not becoming hidden visibility, and so we're back into the ODR issue.  It seems to me that the same issue is going to apply under appleclang for `get_internals()` when compiled not under `-fvisibility=hidden`.

What if we just force hidden visibility for everything under the `pybind11` namespace?  I tested with changing the `NAMESPACE_BEGIN` definition to `namespace name __attribute__((visibility("hidden"))) {`, which seems to fix clang on OS X.

Obviously we'd need to rename the macro (e.g. `PYBIND11_NAMESPACE`), and condition on `__GNUG__`, etc.  But would this cause any other problems?
