As for `return_value_policy`: `py::init<>` is just a shortcut for declaring an `__init__`, which doesn't *have* a return value to apply a policy to.  For `.def("AllocateAnAClass", ...)` it's not going to help: when a pointer is returned that pybind already knows about (which will happen in this case -- because the returned object is already wrapped in another pybind-managed object) we just incref the other object.

One other point, in passing: `super().__init__()` is likely to get you into trouble if you have multiple inheritance happening (in particular, multiple inheritance from Python of multiple wrapped C++ base classes).  This is unfortunately, but Python's MRO is fundamentally incompatible with C++: there's no simple way to work around it to force the MRO into a C++-compatible base class initialization chain.  You're definitely safer sticking to `Base.__init__(self)`.  (Though if you don't have multiple inheritance you don't need to worry, but something to be aware of).

The problem is that the following. The store takes a while to get around to processing the manager's `Create` call because it is busy processing a get request from the driver for 10 million objects.

**Possible solution**
- Large **get** requests to the store should probably be broken down into smaller requests (e.g., perhaps 10000 IDs at a time).
- It shouldn't be necessary to address this problem, but large **wait** and **fetch** requests to the manager should probably be broken down as well.

Where should we break the request into smaller pieces? There are a couple of options.
1. In Python
2. In the plasma client
3. In the store and in the manager

> I'm a tad unsure on the abspath vs path change. Can you add more detail on that in the commit message? I think I know why it's OK in blame (cwd=root vs not), but not in blame -> migration.

In blame data, `path` is the path relative to CWD and `abspath` is path relative to the root of the repo. With CWD==root, they're the same and removed `abspath` to reduce the number of different _paths_ used in the code.

> The other question I have is whether we should first collect all the migration contexts, then gather all files, and run blame once?

Annotating now takes under a second which I think is good enough. The code is cleaner now when annotating happens for each migration context separately. Also, batching blames would only help us in the scenario when we run multiple migrations touch the same files... and it would likely shave off a fraction of a second per locale or so. Let's keep this simple for now and revisit as a potential perf optimization if we hit another bottleneck.

Making 'name' and index field means you can't reference it as a pandas field, so this would no longer work:
```
products = dc.list_products()
products[products['name'] == 'ls5_nbar_albers']
```

That said, measurements is mess. It is really hard to use the multi-index of `(product, measurement)`, and we had to duplicate the index's `measurement` field as a normal pandas field (`name`) so that indexing would work.

Being able to search for products using fields (which the index now supports, but didn't when we made list_products) solves these problems.

As it is being used, Pandas isn't doing a good job at displaying the products and measurements table: truncating fields if they are too long, and hiding rows if there are too many.  We could use a custom renderer function `_repr_html_` to overcome this in notebooks.

With the current `MultiError` design, the `catch` handler never gets passed `MultiError` objects. I'm not sure what you saw that makes you think otherwise. Currently, the handler gets called on all the basic exception objects, so e.g. in `MultiError([Cancelled(), ValueError()])`, the `_exc_filter` in `CancelScope` would get called twice: one with `Cancelled` and once with `ValueError`. So the `isinstance` check you're seeing is to ignore the `ValueError`. The original idea for this issue was to simply move this check into `MultiError.catch`, since otherwise every handler ends up reimplementing it.

With the "MultiError v2" proposal (#611), this changes slightly, and the exception type becomes more important. This proposal isn't implemented yet. But here, the idea is `MultiError.catch(RuntimeError, handler)` takes whatever exception comes out of the code block, and then splits it into two halves: the `RuntimeError` half and the non-`RuntimeError` half. This uses the `split` operation described in #611. And then it calls the handler on the `RuntimeError` half. This might be a simple `RuntimeError`, or it might be a `MultiError`... but if it's a `MultiError`, then it's one that only contains `RuntimeError`s. So again, the handler doesn't have to check the exception type explicitly: it knows that if it's running at all, it's because there was at least one `RuntimeError`.

Q: is the program that starts the "Workflow" span long running, or does it exit quickly? If the latter, it's possible that the tracer doesn't have time to flush the spans.

2 - why do you need to fill the gaps? The messaging use case is naturally async, it's not unusual that you will have gaps in the timeline. The distance between the spans should reflect the queue wait. The only way to fill the gaps would be to instrument the queue broker so that it emits an additional span from receiving the msg to handing it out to the consumer.

3 - indeed, I recently ran into a similar scenario with long running workflows. Unless you have some sort of higher-level operation that keeps an open span for all that time, there's no way today to model the full workflow as a span. It could be done in systems based on X-Trace event model (like Facebook's Canopy). In Jaeger it would require an ability to submit in-progress spans to the backend (see https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger/issues/677)

It's important that we are extremely consistent with regard to output, and very flexible for input. I think that it's very likely that a user will give a single file name, eg:

```
dicom_file = "so-amazing.dcm"
dothething(dicom_file)

and then for larger operations, a list.
dicom_files = ["so-amazing1.dcm", "so-amazing2.dcm"]
dothething(dicom_files)
```
And for both these use cases, the function should work equivalently and the user should get the same (consistent) output. Since we are dealing with functions that (typically) operate on multiple things (lists) I would choose the more consistent of the two (returning a list always). I could see an argument for returning the same format that was input (eg, if a single item is input, parse as list but then return back as single result) but I don't see the good rationale for having an extra error thrown if the input is slightly different. Could you explain why you think this is important?

On adding annotations - it sounds reasonable to me. Maybe call `name` to be `standard_meaning` and instead of enum we can put string there and have some predefined constants in the proto file (for expandable enums it seems more convenient).

My concern though is that then Conv op (and similar ones) effectively changes semantics. Right now the semantics are the input is NxCxD1xD2x... which would not be the case for NHWC. To be sensible I think we need to define annotation on the operator level in one of the ways:
1) Have a separate ConvChannelLast op (ugly)
2) Have an attribute `channel_last` or `channel_axis` on the op that defaults to false/1
3) Have an attribute `data_order` which is not very generic

Then backend implementations can go and complain if the unexpected attribute value is encountered.

Even if we introduce this, I think having input annotations on dimensions is a good idea.


However, I want to better understand which operations are causing trouble for you. With sufficiently smart graph rewriter one should be able to completely propagate layout change through the model with no Transposes left. Recent trend so far has been to adopt NCHW in newer solutions. Maybe we should build sufficiently smart graph transformer.
